or 	translation 	of any 	part 	of this 	work 	including 	oral presentations 	at 	the Academy, written 	material 	in 	this
Manual and accompanying written material, beyond that pennitted 	by 	Section 107 	or 	108 	of the United
States 	Copyright Act, without express penn iss ion 	is 	unlawfuJ, and may be subject to civil litigation 	and
criminal 	prosecution. 	Rights to 	reporting 	on 	the 	Academy 	are 	also reserved. Pennission requests 	should 	be
directed to: Kennedy 	Inner 	Circle Inc., 5818 N. 	7th 	Str. 	#103, 	Phoenix, 	Arizona 85014. Fax 	602-269-3113.
(2): The Academy presentations and material are intended to provide generaJ inforntation 	in 	regard 	(only)
to the specific subject matter 	covered, 	and 	are provided 	with 	the 	understanding that 	the authors, speakers
and 	providers 	of this material 	are 	not 	engaged in rendering legal, accounting 	or 	financia1 	advice, 	and
further, that much 	of this content 	reflects 	opinion. The authors, speakers and providers 	of the Academy
accept 	no 	liability 	or 	responsibility 	whatsoever for 	any decisions, investments, actions, 	etc. 	taken 	by
attendees/participants 	as 	a result 	of their interpretation 	of this content, and attendees specifically indemnify
the authors, speakers and providers from such liability. No warrantees 	of any 	kind 	have 	been made. (3):
Attendees/participants understand their names, infonnation about them 	and 	their questions, comments and
discussions made during the Academy may 	be 	subsequently used in recordings and 	publications 	based 	on
the 	Academy 	and promotion 	of same. (4): Any attendee not 	in 	agreement 	to 	these terms 	and 	conditions
should 	exit the Academy after turning 	in 	all materials.

-- 3 of 253 --

DIS 	:NO 	cellphones 	or 	other 	mobile 	commm1ication 	devices. 	NO 	texting.
Disruptions 	made 	by 	such 	devices 	will 	be 	tnet 	with 	$100.00 	fmes, due and 	payable
itnn1ediately. 	No 	joke.
COMFORT: 	If you 	are too wann, take 	something off. 	If too 	chilly, 	put 	sotnething 	on. 	Do
NOT 	fiddle 	with 	thermostats 	or 	whine 	and 	complain. 	If 	you 	can't 	see 	well, 	at 	frrst
opportunity, 	move. 	Beyond 	that, please, 	no 	pee-ing 	and 	moaning about 	trivia 	Time
here is 	too 	short, 	there's 	too 	much 	to 	cover, 	and there are important 	n1atters 	at hand,
potentially 	affecting 	the quality 	of the 	rest 	of your 	life. 	What does it 	matter 	if you 	must
perch 	on 	an 	uncomfortable 	boulder 	in 	a 	chilly 	drizzle 	to 	get 	it?
BREAKS: 	IT 	IS 	EXTRE~1EL Y IMPORTANT- as 	courtesy 	to 	others 	and 	for 	your 	benefit
- 	not 	to 	dawdle 	during 	breaks, 	return 	late, 	or 	require 	rounding 	up 	and 	herding 	like 	errant
cattle. 	If's 	annoying 	to 	us 	and 	should 	be 	embarrassing 	to 	you. 	Please 	get 	to 	your seats 	on
time.
BEING 	OFF 	DED: 	You 	will 	undoubtedly 	imd 	reason 	to be offended at 	some 	point(s)
during the 	journey. 	It's 	your right 	to 	be as 	offended 	as 	often 	as 	you like, as much 	as 	you
like. 	But 	know 	that 	there's 	no 	profit 	in 	it for you. 	Try 	to 	stay focused 	on 	your reasons 	for
being 	.	here, 	and 	on 	securing as much useful 	information 	as 	possible. 	Further, 	recognize
that 	what 	makes 	you most uncomfortable 	or 	that 	you 	have 	a knee-jerk, 	instant 	(ie.
thoughtless) 	adverse 	reaction 	to 	may, 	in 	fact, 	be 	the 	thing 	you 	most 	need 	to 	think 	most
about.
~ ..
A FEW 	RESOURC ~ES 	will 	be 	offered 	to 	yo~ 	at 	discounts.UNIQUE 	TO 	YOU 	and 	this
occasion. 	There 	is 	no 	obligation to 	accept 	any 	of 	them. 	Being 	grumpy 	about 	them 	being
offered 	is 	foolish 	and 	petty. 	Every 	promise 	about the 	content 	of the 	Academy- 	and 	then
some 	- 	is 	being 	met, 	but 	there 	are 	extensions 	of some 	of 	that 	content 	that 	may 	interest
some 	attendees 	and 	not 	others, 	and 	discovery 	of extremely 	useful 	tools 	and 	resources
specifically 	supportive 	of those 	interests 	is a 	value 	of being here. 	We 	also 	have 	three
SPONSORS 	who are 	buying 	your lunches 	for 	you, 	and will be making 	brief presentations
of 	their 	own- 	two 	during 	those 	lunch 	breaks, 	one 	in 	the 	morning. 	Should 	you 	prefer 	not
giving 	them 	your 	attention, 	there 	are restaurant choices 	in 	and 	near 	the 	hotel, 	or 	you 	may
come 	late. 	However, 	I 	have 	specifically 	chosen 	and 	invited these Sponsors because 	they
can 	be 	of 	eno1mous 	benefit 	to 	you.
•

-- 5 of 253 --

SATURD.	AY 	NIGHT 	INFORMATION
Optional 	Evening 	at 	Track- 	you need 	to 	get 	a 	pass 	from Denise 	during one 	of the
breaks. 	Do 	NOT 	lose it. 	Be 	sure 	you 	have it with 	you 	when 	you 	leave here Saturday
night. 	It 	covers admission, your Program and buffet dinner 	in 	the Clubhouse .
•
A 	bus 	will leave here and return from 	the 	track at times announced. 	We 	are 	NOT 	doing
head counts 	or 	checking names 	off on 	a list. 	If you 	miss the bus, 	you're 	on 	your 	own.
If you 	prefer 	driving 	your 	own 	auto 	or 	car.. pooling, from hotel: make 	right out 	of hotel area a
bottom 	ofhi11, 	go 	straight 	through first 	traffic light (past Me Donalds), down the big 	hill. 	At 	its
bottom, turn right on 	CanaJ 	Road. Stay 	on 	Canal Road to Alexander; you 	go 	under its 	bridge,
make an immediate left 	to 	loop up onto Alexander, going up its hill. 	At 	light at 	Route 	8 (major
artery), 	make 	right. 	On 	your 	left will first 	be 	the 	Ford plant; next, Northfield Park. 	Enter 	first
entrance and follow 	directions 	to 	Clubhouse 	Parking. 	(You 	can 	get a 	Parking 	Pass 	from 	Denise).
On 	arrival 	at 	track, 	you 	may 	either go directly the escalator to 	the 	Clubhouse 	and
there, 	be directed to 	our 	seating 	area 	OR 	first take 	an 	optional 	barn 	tour 	and 	meet
the 	racehorses. 	If 	going to the barns, 	stay 	on the ground floor 	of the Clubhouse and
gather 	by 	the full-size plastic 	horse 	at 	the foot 	of the escalator. You will 	be 	walking 	to
and from 	the 	barn 	area. Note: you are in the 	bam 	are~ 	at your 	own 	risk. Please be 	alert£
horses 	-they 	have right 	of way, 	everywhere. 	Stay 	together- 	do 	not 	wander 	off..
ABOUT 	.	OUR 	SPONSORS 	- 	Refer 	to 	Rolodex®, 	Section 	7 for 	Contact 	Info
•

copJWriters- 	in 	securing, 	impressing, satisfying 	clients, 	making more money, and 	prodadng
superior 	resalts. 	·

unintentionally- including the first client who 	told 	me I was under-charging; the immortal Gary Halbert; 	legendary
past 	masters- 	notably David 	Ogilvy. 	An~ 	as important, those 	from 	whom I 	learned 	how to 	sell, 	far too many to name

-- 8 of 253 --

THE 	BUSINES$ 	TBIA:WGLB_	-_ J{~y 	''Big" 	Ideas
•One 	not 	very 	valuable 	without 	the 	other
•Attraction 	of 	(good) 	clients 	is 	difficult/costly, 	so 	managing 	for 	maximum 	vaJue 	is 	vital
•Client 	satisfaction, 	retention 	and 	value 	is 	NOT 	controlled 	by 	copywriting 	deliverables
or 	results
Efficacy: Strategy + Cop 	riting
-
•This 	is 	NOT 	a 	copywriting 	seminar, 	so 	information 	about 	that 	will 	be 	incidental
•Fundamental 	premise 	is 	elevation 	of 	status 	and 	role 	from 	copywriter 	to
strategic 	advisor+copywriter
•In 	most 	casesr 	strategy 	is 	much 	more 	significant 	that 	copy 	or 	differential 	in
effectiveness 	of 	copy 	or 	skill 	of 	copywriter
Compensation
•
•Money 	is 	representation 	of 	respect, 	status, 	influence, 	power- 	and 	value ... but 	does
NOT 	"automatically" 	come 	to 	you 	from 	excellence 	in 	core 	competencies, 	service,
passion, 	desire, 	interest 	nor 	is 	it 	"entitlement" 	at 	any 	time, 	for 	any 	reason
•Your 	compensation 	will 	be 	commensurate 	with 	your 	preparedness, 	skill, 	value
provided 	to 	clients 	AND 	your ability 	to 	position 	and 	present 	yourself 	and 	that 	value
persuasively 	AND 	effectively 	manage 	the 	client 	relationship ... but 	governed 	by 	your
thinking 	and 	personal 	behaviors 	with 	regard 	to 	compensation 	(money/wealth)
•There 	is 	no 	point 	engineering 	a 	business, 	equipping 	yourself 	with 	skills, 	or 	investing
yourself 	in 	activities 	or 	relationships 	that 	cannot 	satisfy 	your 	financial 	objectives 	- 	or
deluding 	yourself 	about 	what 	is 	required, 	for 	your 	income 	goals
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

Timidity
Under-price
Better antidotes:
Negotiate
Back 	down

From 	Thesaurus: 	FRAUD. 	Impostor, 	fake, 	sham, 	charlatan,
quack, 	mountebank; 	swindler, 	gonif, 	snake 	oil 	salesman, 	fraudster,
racketeer, 	cheat, 	scam 	artist.
Common Antidotes
Avoidance
Timidity
Under-price
Better Antidotes
Practical:
Smart 	selection
Negotiate
Back down
Seek 	credentials from others
Seek 	permission
Wait
Accurate, fair representation, disclosure
Get.adept a finding ''certain 	win's'' 	for client and
self
Psycho-emotional:
Alter your attitude about this subject
Alter 	your 	attitude about risk and failure

SELECTION
•" Better" 	clients ... the 	best 	your 	copywriting 	ski lis 	and 	knowl~dge 	permit
•Bigger 	opportunities 	- 	so 	they 	can 	do 	more 	and 	spend 	more
•Better 	transaction 	values/customer 	values/profits 	- 	so 	they 	can 	do 	more
and 	spend 	more
•Right 	mindset 	about 	marketing .... a 	"seeker"
•Positive 	mindset 	about 	money
•Evidence 	of 	investments 	in 	advertising, 	marketing
•Avoid 	dumb, 	cheapskate, 	irrational, 	needy, 	etc. 	clients!
•Discipline 	yourself 	against 	"weak 	moments"
YOUR 	SKILLS
1: 	Identifying 	and 	exploiting 	ALL 	the 	opportunities 	in 	a client's 	business
2: 	Marketing 	yourself- 	so 	there's 	sufficient 	"deal 	flow" 	to 	discriminate
3: 	Copywriting
I
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 33 of 253 --

Some 	of the
•
Urgent/pressing 	needs 	(actual)
Needs 	(emotional)- 	the 	way 	not 	having 	work 	feels
Obligation: 	"They've 	been 	with 	me 	since 	the 	beginning ....."
Guilt: 	"My 	mother 	cleaned 	other 	peoples' 	houses 	and 	made 	less 	per
.month 	than 	this 	will 	pay 	for 	a 	day- 	how 	dare 	l .... what 	would 	momma
think?"
Compassion: 	"That's 	all 	they 	can 	pay ... "
Poverly orientation: 	"That's 	all 	they 	will 	pay ... "
Lack 	of confidence 	in 	ability 	or 	appropriateness 	to 	client
Resume-building
The 	cache' 	client
Denial 	of 	what 	this 	business 	really 	is
Desire 	to 	"just 	write"
Poor 	sales 	skills
Start 	'n 	stop 	self-marketing 	(while 	work 	gets 	done)

en 	You 	Can,
Deal 	With 	Smart 	d 	Success 	People.
Be 	Careful 	Not 	To 	Under-
"If 	you 	have a large, perfect diamond and 	you 	take it to the
village marketplace, 	you 	will have a tremendously hard
time 	ing 	to convince 	the 	junk 	peddlers to give 	you 	five
dollars for it. However, 	if 	you 	circumvent 	the village
peddlers and the small-town shopkeepers and go directly to
the 	most 	reputable diamond dealer 	in 	the nation, 	you 	would
not 	have 	to waste 	your 	energy in attempting to convince
him. 	You 	wouldn't 	even need to open 	your 	mouth, and he
might 	offer 	you 	$500,000.00 	for 	it. 	If.you 	are 	a 	ttue 	.gem~
9o 	n~t 	waste 	your. _energy 	trying 	to. 	unQ~rsell 	yourself to 	th~
Common. 	village 	J?eddl~rs. 	·Y ou.. will 	onlY 	annOY 	them 	and
waste 	yqw 	time. 	It 	is 	much 	easier 	to 	sell 	valuable 	things 	to
people 	who 	recognize 	the 	value 	than 	those who do not.''
(THICK FACE, BLACK HEART)
1. 	NEVER 	deal with underlings.
2. Deal 	ONLY 	with check-writers and decision-makers.
4. 	If 	a committee is involved, 	refuse to participate. Move on.
•
5. As soon as you detect 	the 	decision-maker is unreasonably cheap,0 	- 	0
move 	on.

•
TERMS 	& 	CONDITIONS 	RE.
CONSULTING 	RELATIONSHIP 	WITH 	DAN 	KENNEDY
1: 	ABSENCE 	OF 	WARRANTIES .	: (a) 	Consultant 	offers 	no 	warranty 	of non-disclosure 	or 	confidentiality.
WhiJe 	Consu ltant 	endeavors 	to 	respect 	Clienfs 	infonnation, 	Consultant 	.	is 	privy 	to 	far 	too 	much 	duplicative 	or
similar 	ideas 	and 	information 	from 	multiple 	sources 	to 	enter 	into 	any 	commitments 	regarding 	confidentiality.
(b) 	Client 	is 	solely 	and 	exclusively 	responsible 	for 	whatever 	use 	Client 	may 	make 	of advice, 	suggestions,
ideas 	and 	informat ion 	provided 	by 	Consultant. 	Both 	parties 	acknowledge 	that 	ConsuHant 	cannot 	be
expected 	to 	be 	familiar 	with 	the 	legal 	aspects of the 	Client's 	particular 	business. 	(c) 	ConsuHant 	offers 	no
warranties 	regarding 	accuracy 	or 	efficacy 	of 	advice 	or 	information 	provided. 	(d) 	Under 	no 	circumstances 	can
Consultant 	be 	held 	liable 	by 	Client 	or 	parties 	related 	to 	Client 	in 	any 	way, 	for 	any 	reason, 	for 	an 	amount
exceeding 	compensation 	paid 	by 	Client to 	Consultant
2: 	INDEMNITY: 	(a) 	Client 	indemnifies 	Consultant 	against any 	and 	all 	claims 	or 	liabilities 	arising 	from 	second
parties, 	outside 	parties, 	shareholders 	or 	others 	holding 	interest 	in 	Client's 	company(ies) 	resulting 	from 	Client
making 	approved 	or 	unapproved 	use 	of its 	relationship 	with 	Consultant for 	promotional 	or 	other purposes 	or
in 	any 	way 	related 	to 	Consultant's 	advice 	to 	Client. 	(b) 	Should 	Consultant 	be 	named 	in 	any 	regulatory
agency 	or 	other 	legal 	actions 	filed 	against 	Client, 	Client 	warrants 	it 	shall 	exert all 	possible 	efforts 	to 	have
Consultant 	severed 	and 	released 	from 	such 	actions, 	co-operate 	with 	Consultant's separate 	counsel's 	efforts
to 	effect such 	severance, 	and 	bear or reimburse 	Consultant's 	Jegal 	costs 	incurred 	in 	effecting 	such
severance 	and/or in 	defending 	against 	the 	action, 	with 	Consultant 	retaining 	right 	of choice 	of its 	counsel.
3: 	ACCESS: 	Client 	understands 	that 	Consultant 	is 	rarely 	available 	on 	short 	notice, 	and 	messages or 	FAXes
may 	be 	answered 	after 	several 	days. 	and 	telephone 	conferences 	require 	advance 	scheduling. 	Should 	in-
person 	meetings 	and/or extended 	telephone 	discussions 	be 	required 	by 	Clien~ 	that are 	not expressly
provided 	for 	in 	other 	agreements 	or correspondence 	between 	Consultant and 	Client, 	Consultanfs 	published
hourly 	or 	daily 	fees 	may 	apply.
4: 	CONFLICTS 	OF 	INTEREST: 	(a)Vendor 	recommendations 	may 	be 	made 	by 	Consultant to 	Client
involving 	vendors 	in 	which 	Consultant 	has 	ownership 	or 	interest or 	from 	which 	Consultant 	receives 	fees,
commissions 	or 	other 	compensation. 	Such 	details 	may 	not 	be 	disclosed. 	(b) 	ConsuHant 	makes 	no 	warranty
of exclusivity 	to 	Client. 	and 	Consultant 	may work 	with 	other clients 	that 	may 	be 	viewed 	by 	Client as
competitive.
IN 	UNDERSTANDING 	AND 	ACCEPTANCE 	OF 	THESE 	TERMS:

TERMS 	& 	CONDITIONS 	RE. 	CONSULTING 	DAY 	WITH 	DAN 	KENNEDY
1: 	SCHEDULE 	CHANGES. 	(a} 	NQ 	c~ncellations 	qr .re-scheduling 	i_sperf11ittedJ 	deposits 	paid 	are 	non-
r~funpabte 	and 	balances 	to 	be 	paid 	on 	consulting 	day 	remain 	due 	even 	if 	Client fails 	to 	complete 	the 	day_a&
scheduled. 	The 	sole 	exceptions 	are 	documented 	medical 	emergency, 	hospitalization, 	severe 	illness 	or
death 	in 	immediate 	family. 	Client 	is advised 	to 	allow ample 	travel 	time 	en-route 	to 	the 	consultation, 	as 	travel
delayed 	or 	disrupted 	by 	weather or other 	reasons 	does 	not 	absolve 	Client 	of these 	financial 	responsibilities.
(b) 	Consultant 	may 	cancel 	or 	re-schedule 	and 	refund 	CUenfs 	deposit wjthout 	flnancial 	penalty 	up 	to 	15 	days
prior 	to 	the 	date, 	or, 	within 	15 	days, 	refund 	deposit plus 	reimburse 	any 	documented, 	non-refundable 	air
travel 	purchased 	by 	Client. 	(Such 	cancellation 	or 	change 	by 	Consultant 	would 	onfy 	occur 	in 	extreme
emergency. 	This 	has 	never occurred 	in 	30 	years.)
2: 	TAPE 	RECORDING 	by 	Client 	is 	encouraged, 	with 	recording 	only 	for 	personal 	and 	private 	use 	of the
Client.
3: 	ABSENCE 	OF 	WARRANTIES: 	(a) 	Consultant 	offers 	no 	warranty of non-disclosure 	or 	confidentiality.
While 	Consultant 	endeavors 	to 	respect 	Client's 	information, 	Consultant is 	privy 	to 	far 	too 	much 	duplicative 	or
similar ideas 	and 	information 	from 	multiple 	sources 	to 	enter 	into 	any 	commitments 	regarding 	confidentiality.
(b) 	Client 	is 	solely 	and 	exctusively 	responsible 	for 	whatever 	use 	Client 	may 	make 	of advice, 	suggestions,
ideas 	and 	information 	provided 	by 	Consultant or 	discussed 	during 	the 	consulting 	day. 	Both 	parties
acknowledge 	that 	Consultant 	cannot be 	expected 	to 	be 	familiar 	with 	the 	legal 	aspects 	of the 	C1ient's
particular 	business. 	(c) 	Consultant offers 	no 	warranties 	regarding 	accuracy or efficacy of advice 	or
information 	provided. 	(d) 	Under no 	circumstances 	can 	Consultant 	be 	held 	liable 	by 	Client 	or 	parties 	related
to 	Client 	in 	any wayJ 	for any 	reason, 	for an 	amount 	exceeding 	compensation 	paid 	by 	Client 	to 	Consultant.
(e) 	For 	purposes 	of this 	Day, 	Consultant 	is 	Kennedy 	Inner 	Circle, 	Inc., 	furnishing 	Dan 	S. 	Kennedy.
4: 	DETAILS 	OF 	DAY. 	Time 	is 	from 	9:00A.M. 	to 	4:00P.M. 	EST. 	Location 	is 	assigned 	to 	individual 	Client,
usually 	either 	at 	Consultanfs 	home 	near 	Cleveland, 	Ohio, 	or 	near 	his 	Reston, 	Virginia 	home. 	If meeting 	in
Ohio, 	Client 	is to 	stay at 	the 	La Quinta 	Inn 	in 	Macedonia, 	Ohio 	and 	will 	be 	picked 	up 	there 	by 	Consultant at
8:30A.M. 	It 	is 	important that 	Client 	be 	checked 	in 	at 	this 	hotei 	the 	night 	before 	the 	Consulting 	Day. 	If
meeting 	in 	Virginia, 	Client 	is 	to 	have 	a hotel 	suite 	or meeting 	room 	specifically 	at 	the 	Hyatt at Reston 	Town
Center or 	a meeting 	room 	at 	the 	Regis 	Office 	Suites 	in 	Reston 	Town 	Center 	(no 	other 	site 	is 	acceptable).
Client 	will 	be 	provided 	with 	additional 	details 	as 	necessary.
5: 	MATERIALS 	FOR 	CONSULT ANT'S 	REVIEW 	IN 	ADVANCE 	should 	be 	in 	the 	Phoenix 	office, 	received 	by
Consultant's 	assistant, 	Vicky 	Tolleson, 	no 	later than 	TEN 	business 	days 	prior to 	the 	Consulting 	Day, 	and
should 	be 	limited 	to 	agenda~ 	outline, 	key 	questions 	and 	items 	planned 	for 	discussion 	and 	only 	essential
reading 	material. 	Do 	NOT 	send 	audio 	CO's 	or 	DVD's 	or 	ask 	Consultant 	to 	visit web 	sites. 	Do 	NOT 	fax 	more
than 	ten 	pages. 	A package 	sent by 	Federal 	Express 	is 	preferred .

-- 72 of 253 --

Sarnple
General 	Questionnaire 	prepared 	for 	use 	by 	Glazer-Kennedy 	Insider's
Circle TM 	Independent 	Business 	Advisors, 	staff. 	and 	me, 	in 	"speed
cntiques" 	. •

www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 73 of 253 --

For
ADVERTISING 	CRITIQUE
With 	Dan 	Kennedy 	Criteria
----------------------------------
General 	Comments
Demonstrates 	good understanding 	of 	direct-response marketing
Clever, 	interesting
Smart 	adaptation 	of 	proven 	ideas, 	themes, 	copy
Bluntly, 	it's 	awful, and 	sho\\'S 	little 	understanding 	of 	our 	most 	basic 	strategies
Basic 	idea, 	pre~ise 	is 	okay, 	but 	needs 	a lot 	of 	work 	·
Headline(s)/Headline Space
Wasted: 	there 	is 	no 	headline
No big, 	bold, exciting promise
Generic, 	could 	apply 	to 	any 	business in 	your 	field
Contains 	no 	news
Vague, 	contains 	no meaningful 	specifics
Fails 	at 	its primary job: 	compelling 	me 	to 	keep 	reading
Good, 	but 	could be better ... could 	be 	strengthened with specifics
Outstanding!
Bad 	Example: 	Malpractice Insurance Solutions
Improved Example: 	New Breakthrough 	'Safe 	Harbor' 	For 	Pennsylvania Doctors:
The 	Cure 	For 	The 	Malpractice Crisis Has Arrived.
Lower 	Premiums, 	Better 	Coverage, More Aggressive Defense,
Guaranteed. 	·
Openings 	For 	Doctors 	To 	Join 	The 	Group 	End 	March 	23.
Testimonials
None. 	Completely absent. 	(A 	Major 	Mistake)
Weak ... no 	specifics
Weak .... unemotional, 	impersonal
Blind ... not 	ID'd 	by 	full name, place, occupation, etc.
Redundant ... too 	many 	making 	same 	point instead 	of 	each making a different
point
Good- 	but 	why 	not 	more?
Outstanding!
Features 	vs. Benefits
(Example: Feature: 	You'll 	own the fastest power drill that can be legally sold. Benefit:
You'll finish 	your job 	in 	half the time.)
All facts 	and 	features, none translated to benefits
Benefits 	are 	vague, unexciting, impersonal
Generic 	statements that could be 	made 	by 	anybody 	in 	your 	category
Insufficient 	quantity 	of 	benefits
Good, 	solid 	job 	of 	presenting benefits
•
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 74 of 253 --

•
Unique 	Selling 	Proposition
(lVhy 	should 	I 	choose 	you 	vs. 	any 	and 	every other option available to 	1ne?)
None. 	You 	are 	''me-too", 	same 	as 	many 	others
Clever 	slogan 	fails 	to 	deliver 	a 	clear 	promise 	of 	benefit
Fails 	to 	differentiate you 	from 	others
On 	the 	right 	track but still need 	work .....
Outstanding! 	(As good 	as: 	fresh, 	hot 	pizza, delivered in 	30 	minutes 	or 	less,I
guaranteed)
Organization
(Object: a greased chute the prospect cannot get 	off of 	..a logical process 	that 	leads toe
the 	inevitable 	conclusion, your desired response.)
•
Style
Does 	not 	follow 	a proven sales 	formula 	(see: Ultimate Sales Letter book)
Confusing, meandering
Needs 	a 	summary
Nicely organized
Too 	stiff, fortnal, institutional
Company/product 	focused rather 	than 	customer 	focused
I/ we 	instead 	of 	you
Not 	friendly, 	conversationalt 	written 	as 	you 	and 	they 	talk
Loaded with techno-babble 	or 	proprietary tertninology
Lacks 	emotion
Dull, 	boring .... reading 	must 	be 	fun
Reads well ... .like conversational selling 	in 	print- 	nice 	job!
Offer 	& 	Call To Action
None.
Plain 	vanilla, ordinary, unexciting ... no 	one's 	pants are 	on 	fire 	to 	respond 	to 	this
Unclear. 	Confusing. Confused 	readers 	do 	nothing.
Not 	clear 	the 	benefits 	of 	doing 	what 	you 	ask 	me 	to 	do.
Insufficient 	or 	no 	urgency
No 	deadline
Urgency 	not 	made 	real, believable
Insufficient reward, incentive, 	bonus 	for acting quickly
Insufficient loss, penalty, 	fear 	of 	loss 	for 	not 	acting quickly• •
Good, 	complete call to action!
Guarantee
None
Weak, 	wimpy, 	ordinary, 	unimaginative
Lacks 	specifics
Sabotaged 	by 	weasel clauses
Multiple 	guarantee vs. single guarantee 	would 	be 	an 	improvement
Terrific! Strong, straightforward, interesting.
-
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 75 of 253 --

•
Format 	I 	Media
Inappropriate 	For 	Task. 	(Egs.: 	Postcard 	advertising 	special 	events 	at 	a 	local 	mall
sent 	to 	area 	homes, 	fine. 	Postcard 	sent 	to 	doctors 	about 	advanced 	laser 	equipment,
wrong. 	Gatekeepers 	throw 	out 	such 	'junk 	mail'. 	Size 	of 	transaction 	warrants
more 	elaborate 	mailing.
Ordinary 	and 	mundane. 	Your 	prospects 	see 	a bazillion 	of 	these 	and 	they all
look 	the 	same.
Be 	better 	served 	by 	a full- length 	sales 	letter.
Envelope 	is 	"'half-pregnanf''- 	either 	be 	IOOo/	o 	"sneak 	up" 	or 	if 	you 	reveal 	it 	is
commercial 	mail 	at 	all, 	might 	as 	well 	use 	all 	the 	space, pile 	on 	"teaser 	copy",
photos, 	etc.
Cosmetics
You've 	made 	a 	letter 	look 	like 	an 	ad 	or 	brochure. 	Switch 	to 	Courier 	or 	similar
typeface 	(not 	a 	"block'' 	type), 	indent 	paragraphs
Break 	up 	copy 	with 	subheads 	(written 	like 	headlines)
Use 	more 	boldface 	type, italics, underlining, different 	type 	sizes 	to 	help 	reader
move 	a1ong; 	to 	substitute 	for 	voice 	inflection 	and 	gestures
Too 	pretty, 	too 	slick ... .looks 	like 	big 	corporation's 	standard 	fare
Misc. Ideas, Tips, Notes
Consider 	gift-with-appointment 	strategy 	to 	help this 	offer
You've 	got 	a 	free 	offer 	"buried" 	-tell 	me 	right 	up 	top 	there's 	something 	free
Give 	more 	ways 	to 	respond
Consider 	adding 	a 	''grabber" 	or 	lumpy 	object, 	and 	tying 	theme 	to 	it
("As 	you 	can 	see, 	I've 	enclosed 	a 	bag 	of 	M&lvi's. 	Why 	have 	I 	done 	this ... ")
Tell 	more 	of 	your 	story. 	Make 	this 	personal.
The 	stories/ examples 	used 	are 	too 	long, 	the 	reader 	gets 	lost ... tighten 	them.
Too 	many 	"bullet 	points'', 	benefits .. 	.list 	this 	long 	disrupts 	the 	reader's 	movement
down 	the 	greased 	chute. Pull 	them 	out 	into 	a separate 	enclosure.
You 	may 	have 	a 	list 	or 	media 	problem 	more 	than a 	message 	problem
Answers 	To 	Questions
NO, 	there's 	no 	significant 	differences 	between 	B2B 	and 	consumer 	advertising,
marketing, 	direct-mail, etc., 	and 	insisting 	there 	is leads 	most 	B2B 'ers 	to 	put 	out
dull, boring, 	uninteresting 	pablum 	everybody 	ignores. 	B2B 	is 	a 	misnomer.
Businesses 	don,t 	buy 	things. 	Only 	people 	buy 	things.
Your 	expectations 	were/are 	wholly 	unreasonable. 	Forget 	about 	response
percentage. 	Think 	return 	on 	invested 	dollars.
Way 	too 	small 	of 	a 	test 	to 	judge 	anything.
Resources 	Relevant 	To You
Glazer/Kennedy 	Inner 	Circle 	Membership 	including 	No 	B.S. 	Marketing 	Letter*
Ultimate 	Sales 	Letter 	book
Ultimate 	Marketing 	Plan 	book
No 	B.S. 	Direct 	Marketing 	book
No 	B.S. 	Sales 	Success 	book

-- 105 of 253 --

ECESSARY 	ATTITUDES
ROLE
"Service"= 	Hservant". 	Not 	your role 	nor 	way 	to 	be 	of 	greatest 	value 	to 	client 	You 	are
his 	coach/consultant, 	not 	his 	servant. 	You 	are 	not 	there 	to 	be 	of service. You are there to
achieve 	certain results requiring his 	compliance 	and 	your 	exercise 	of control 	over 	·him.
You 	are 	the 	superior, 	not 	the 	lackey.
SUPERIO •
Recognition 	that 	they are 	blind 	though 	they 	can 	see; weak, hamstnmg or overwhelmed
although successful ..... and 	in certain 	ways, 	you 	are 	smarter, clearer thinking, stronger,
faster, superior. 	Notbjng 	is 	more 	useful to the consultant 	than 	the 	realization 	that
everyone 	is a 	bumbling 	idiot 	(at 	least 	part 	of the 	time, 	at 	least with some
things) .... disdain 	is 	a source 	of 	power.
CONFIDENCE •
Deutsch's 	book: 	OFTEN 	V/RONG, 	NEVER 	IN 	DOUBT. 	We 	sell 	certainty. 	Even 	giant
look 	for someone 	to 	tell 	them 	what 	to 	do. 	Landis' 	'~stern 	but 	loving parent."
MONEY
"\Vhen 	they show up, 	bill 	'em." 	You 	n1ust 	decide 	to 	make a 	lot 	of money, 	to 	refuse 	to
"walk 	across the street" for less 	than 	your 	rates, to 	put 	an 	end 	to 	dispensing 	an}1hing 	for
free.
VALUE
Not 	related 	to 	time 	(hourly 	wage 	worker 	thinking) or 	bulk 	(Costco 	· 	· 	g).
Not 	necessarily related 	to 	your 	(superior) 	lmowledge 	or 	expertise either.
Value 	is 	in 	actual/potential value 	of results 	to 	client.
EXERCISE 	OF 	POWER, 	COMFORTA,BL~ 	WITH
Consulting/coaching is 	about 	the 	subtle exercise 	of power 	over 	the 	client, 	through 	means
including psychological 	manipulation. 	If you 	are 	not 	comfortable with exercising power,
you 	will fail 	in 	this field.
•
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 116 of 253 --

-
Many 	clients 	obsess 	mostly, unreasonably 	over this. 	Most-
consultants/copywriters cannot provide 	exclusivity 	and
should not consent to 	non-disclosure 	or 	confidentiality
agreements, 	so 	there's 	conflict.
Whether 	willing 	to admit or not, most 	clients 	are 	very 	aware
of, and often, embarrassed and frustrated 	by 	their 	own
limitations ... and, often, 	in 	the 	selling 	of
consulting/copywriting, the 	client's 	inadequacies are
brought 	into 	spotlight. 	The 	more 	dysfunctional the client,
the 	greater 	his concern 	that 	he'll 	be wasting 	money 	because
of his/his 	company's own 	inability to contribute to the
process 	and/or 	successfully 	implement to 	obtain 	best
results.
Clients 	fear 	the 	Therapist 	Syndrome 	and never getting
well 	... although, at same time, they fear being deserted 	by
the provider of services prematurely.
-

who 	lived 	down 	the 	street 	from 	us. 	He 	was called 	''The 	Animal'' 	and he

Begin 	by 	being 	nown 	for
Something
"In 	the 	beginning, 	you 	must work 	to 	establish 	a
reputation 	foro~~ 	outstanding 	quality, 	whether
generosity or cunning. This 	quality 	(needs 	to) 	set 	you
apart 	and 	get 	other 	people 	to 	talk 	about 	you. 	You 	then
make 	your reputation 	known 	to 	as 	many 	people 	as
possible, 	subtly 	at 	first ....then 	watch 	as 	it 	spreads ... "
(48 Laws)
I started with and quickly abandoned 'personal
development.' 	I focused 	on 	direct-response advertising, 	then
direct response 	marketing, 	building a 	reputation 	as 	someone
with 	unique 	abilities 	in 	that area. 	I built 	it 	in. a target market
(speakers), 	then 	with 	a 	broader 	client/customer 	base
(entrepreneurs), 	and 	took 	care to build 	it 	most 	convincingly
and frrmly within 	my 	own 	herd. 	Only 	after it was solidly
established, did I start expanding with rings around 	it,
fuzzying its edges 	and 	claiming 	more territory as 	my 	own.
You 	move 	from N 	~--- 	OW 	to 	BROAD 	toN 	~~o-.A--. 	OW 	to
BROAD

-- 127 of 253 --

In 	the 	mid-1980's, some researchers at Cleveland 	State
University made a 	startling discovery.
•
The 	researchers created for 	two 	fictitious job 	candidates-
Dave 	and John 	two identical resumes, and two 	almost
identical letters 	of 	reference. The 	only 	difference 	was 	that
John's 	letter included the sentence:•
''Sometimes, 	John 	can 	be 	difficult 	to 	get along 	with."
The 	researchers showed the resumes to personnel directors .
•
Which 	candidate did the directors most want to interview?
•
-
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 128 of 253 --

4-16
On 	April15", 	tax 	day, 	I was 	too 	busy 	to 	be 	aggravated 	by 	it. 	And 	I 	pay 	my 	taxes 	in
August, 	anyway. 	April 	15th 	was 	the 	day 	of 	the 	month 	for 	(nearly) 	all 	my 	phone
appointments 	for 	the 	month. 	People 	are 	curious 	about 	this, 	so 	I 	thought 	I'd 	give 	you•
a 	.
8- 	8:30AM: 	I 	was 	interviewed 	by 	Larry 	Parman, 	for 	contribution 	to 	his 	book- 	which
will. 	of 	course, 	eventually 	serve 	as 	promotion 	for 	my 	books. 	8:30-9 	AM: 	coaching
call 	with 	one 	of 	my 	private 	clients, 	Mike 	Storms. 	9:05- 	9:25: 	regular 	monthly
coaching 	call 	with 	a 	member 	of 	my 	Private 	Client 	Group, 	Dennis 	Tubbergen. 	9:30-
10:30: 	•attended" 	the 	Kennedy's 	Barber 	Clubs 	Board 	meeting, 	as 	advisor. 	10:35-
10:55: 	regular 	monthly 	coaching 	call 	with 	a member 	of 	my 	Private 	Client 	Group,
Craig 	Proctor. 	11:00-12:30: 	was 	a guest 	on 	Chris 	Cardell's 	monthly 	tale-seminar 	for
his 	mastermind 	group 	in 	the 	U.K. 	12:30-1 :00: 	break. 	1:00-2:30: 	on 	the 	monthly 	tela-
class 	for 	Glazer-Kennedy 	Insider's 	Circle's 	Diamond 	Members. 	2:30-4:00: 	monthly
consulting 	call 	with 	Bill 	Glazer. 	4:00-4:20: 	regular 	monthly 	coaching 	call 	with 	a
member 	of 	my 	Private 	Client 	Group, 	Chris 	Cardell. 	4:30- 	5:00: 	coaching 	call 	with
one 	of 	my 	private 	clients, 	Michael 	Kimble. 	5:05-6:05: 	guest 	on 	a tale-seminar 	for
Brian 	Kaskavalciyan's 	client 	group 	in 	the 	home 	remodeling 	industry. 	6:05-6:20:
phone 	appointment 	with 	Shannon 	McCarthy 	re. 	upcoming 	seminar. 	6:20-6:30: 	call
to 	Vicky 	at 	my 	office.
•
Regrettably, 	I 	didn't 	get 	every 	necessary 	phone 	meeting 	for 	the 	entire 	month
squeezed 	into 	this 	one 	day, 	and 	have 	had 	and 	will 	have 	five 	or 	six 	other 	phone
appointments 	scattered 	throughout 	the 	month. 	But 	all 	but 	those 	few, 	done 	this 	day.
Sure, 	I 	recognize 	you 	probably, 	legitimately 	can't 	so 	tightly 	restrict 	access 	to 	you 	and
restrict 	your 	communication 	with 	clients 	and 	others. 	But, 	compare 	this 	to 	most, 	who
live 	at 	the 	opposite 	extreme: 	constantly, 	incessantly, 	indiscriminately, 	and 	randomly 	if
not 	chaotically 	accessible 	and 	~ccessing 	from 	dawn 	to 	sleep 	every 	day. 	via 	multiple
mobile 	devices 	with 	them 	as 	extra 	appendages; 	interrupted, 	interfered 	with 	and
buffeted 	about 	by 	others' 	whims, 	impulses, 	demands 	as 	well 	as 	their 	own 	entirely
invited 	distractions. 	Is 	it 	any 	wonder 	they 	can't 	get 	anything 	done? 	What 	they 	get
done 	is 	wrong 	or 	ineffective 	much 	of 	the 	time? 	They 	"don't 	have 	time• 	to 	read
~ 	~ 	lly 	chosen, 	valuable 	information 	from 	quality 	sources? 	Their 	brains 	turning 	to
mush? •
1also 	acknowledge 	most 	lack 	my 	stamina. 	If 	you 	cou~t, 	the 	day 	~~bed 	above 	had
five 	5 	minute 	breaks, 	one 	10 	minute 	break, 	one 	30 	m1nute 	break 	Within 	10-1/2 	hours.
-
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 129 of 253 --

And 	I 	was 	writing 	for 	an 	hour 	before 	those 	calls 	started. 	But, 	I'm 	not 	super-human.
I'm 	not 	even 	an 	athlete. 	Candidly, 	I couldn't 	do 	multiple 	days 	of 	this 	back 	to 	back
anymore. 	The 	mind's 	okay 	but 	the 	body 	gives 	out. 	But 	the 	alternative 	most 	accept 	is
to 	scatter 	all 	these 	calls 	and 	activities 	throughout 	their 	entire 	month, 	each 	one 	most
certainly 	consuming 	at 	least 	50°/o 	more 	time 	absent 	the 	forced 	endings 	by 	next 	calls'
starts, 	plus 	the 	lost 	time 	to 	getting 	back 	on 	track 	with 	whatever 	was 	befng 	done
before 	the 	call 	or 	is 	being 	started 	after 	it. 	When 	I work, 	I 	work. 	Straight-through. 	And
I 	would 	work 	in 	the 	same 	way 	if, 	for 	example, 	making 	presentat ions 	to 	new 	patients;
they'd 	all 	be 	clumped 	into 	one 	to 	several 	afternoons 	and 	evenings 	during 	the 	month,
not 	scattered 	about 	all 	over 	the 	calendar. 	Regimen 	improves 	focus, 	concentration
and 	productivity; 	spontaneity 	or 	randomness 	does 	not. 	Indulge 	your 	lust 	for
spontaneity 	on 	your 	off 	days. 	As 	Winget 	says, 	it's 	called 	'work' 	for 	a reason. 	And
don't 	give 	me 	the 	"creativity" 	argument,either. 	It's 	b.s., 	too. 	The 	most 	heralded,
celebrated, 	successful 	writers, 	artists, 	etc. 	worked/work 	by 	regimen. 	Not 	waiting 	for
random 	creative 	impulse. 	1'm 	not 	going 	to 	take 	time 	to 	Jook 	it 	up; 	t 	think 	it 	was 	Oscar
Wilde: 	''I 	write 	only 	when 	inspired. 	Fortunately 	for 	my 	creditors, 	I 	am 	inspired 	every
morning 	precisely 	at 	8:00A.M." 	·
Most 	people 	place 	themselves 	in 	disadvantageous 	situations 	all 	the 	time, 	then
unreasonably 	expect 	peak 	performance 	and 	successful 	results. 	They 	not 	only
diffuse 	focus 	and 	sabotage 	concentration, 	and 	let 	each 	task 	consume 	more 	time
than 	needed 	through 	rando ~mness 	vs. 	clumping, 	they 	make 	important 	calls 	while
driving, 	walking 	on 	crowded 	streets, 	eating, 	peeing; 	they 	respond 	to 	correspondence
with 	instant 	ie. 	thoughtless 	communication; 	they 	write 	on 	their 	laptops 	or 	i-pads, 	etc.,
at 	Starbucks 	away 	from 	all 	resources 	that 	are 	needed 	to 	do 	a 	good 	job; 	and 	on 	and
on 	and 	on. 	Salespeople 	sell 	to 	poorly 	prepared 	prospects 	in 	unfavorable
en vi ron ments. 	This 	Iist 	is 	Jong, 	long, 	long.
Anyway, 	there's 	a 	"phone 	day."
•
•
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

Thomas 	Edison 	has a 	reputation 	as a great scientist and an
incredibly 	persistent 	one, 	but 	there 	is significant historical
evidence 	that 	a 	number 	of 	his 	experiments actually 	were
quick, 	based 	on 	other little-known scientists' research. 	In
reality, 	h~ 	\¥as 	_as 	great_ 	a 	s~lesman 	as 	inventor, 	m·ost 	ad~pt 	at
raising 	~ndless 	Gapi!fl.l 	fron1 	investQrs. 	To 	do 	so~ 	he 	w_qul_d
design 	'risu~lly, 	dazzling 	experiments 	presented 	in 	formal
settin 	s 	with 	investors 	and 	II!edia 	resent. 	At 	those
presentations, 	he 	talked 	about 	fascinating 	applications 	_such
as 	rol)o~$~ 	~bich 	he 	actually had 	no_ 	i;t).tent 	w4,_at~o~ver. 	of
spending 	any 	time 	or 	money 	working on. His great rival,
Nikola 	Tesla, 	may 	have 	been 	a 	more 	brilliant inventor, and
Edison 	may 	have 	purloined 	ideas from him, 	but 	Edison
defmitely 	Vlent 	to great lengths to keep 	Tesla 	out 	of 	the
limelight 	including 	turning 	down 	the 	Nobel 	Prize 	in 	1915,
to 	be 	shared 	with 	Tesla. 	Without 	the 	more 	famous Edison,•
•
the 	prize com.mittee 	gave 	it 	to 	neither. 	Point 	is, 	Edison 	was at
least as 	much 	a 	Barnum 	or 	a Trump as an inventor.

Everen
Freelance
SIOPP 	sales
3.6 	mi
r 	surpasses 	150 	m 	on 	pieces•
' n 	paid 	orders ... and 	still 	counting•
en 	I 	first 	started 	in 	my 	role
as creative director for 	one 	of
the 	most 	recognized 	and 	most
successful companies 	in
direct 	marketing, I made 	one 	mistake."
I 	selected freelance copywriters 	based 	on
their samples. 	Wrong!
Of course 	it's 	important 	to 	see 	any
writer's 	samples. 	But 	what matters 	most
is 	response 	in 	the 	mailbox - consistently
over 	time - 	for 	one 	mailer after another.
With 	apologies 	to 	Vince Lombardi, 	one
of the 	winningest 	football coaches 	ever 	-
Response 	isn't 	everything,
it's the 	only 	thing.
So 	I'll 	be 	all 	too 	happy 	to 	show 	you 	my
samples before 	you 	hire 	me 	to write 	your
next 	direct 	response control.
But 	don't 	just 	ask 	me 	or 	any 	writer 	for
samples 	of our 	printed 	or online 	copy 	-
ask for 	the 	results.
My 	samples 	probably 	don't 	look 	much
like 	your 	current 	controL 	The 	products
and 	services 	I've 	sold, 	though 	widely
varied, 	may 	not 	be 	very 	similar 	to 	yours.
You 	may 	or 	may 	not 	think 	my 	copy 	is
wildly 	creative. 	That's 	okay. I 	don't
Imow 	about 	your 	bank, 	but 	they 	don't
take 	creativity for deposit 	do'Wll 	at 	rnine.
The 	only thing 	any 	marketer 	can 	take 	to
the 	bank 	is 	paid 	response.
Direct 	mail 	results 	on 	page one
of 	the 	Wall Street 	Journal
Did 	anyone 	ever 	ask 	you 	how 	you 	got
started 	in 	direct mail? 	It's 	a cotnmon
question, since 	direct 	tnail 	is 	not
something 	they 	teach 	in 	school 	(at 	least
not 	when I went to 	school.)
I 	got started in direct marketing at 	the
agency created 	by 	the 	man 	often called
the 	father 	(or 	godfather) 	of political
•
direct mail, Richard 	A. 	Viguerie.
Richard's 	company 	hired 	me 	at 	age 	24
because I 	had 	politics 	coursing 	through
my 	veins. 	Then 	Richard 	and 	the other
direct 	mail 	pros 	at 	the 	agency 	set 	out to
make 	a copywriter 	out 	of me.
I 	rewrote 	my 	frrst letter 	half 	a dozen times
before 	my 	immediate boss finally sent it
up to Richard 	for 	review. And 	it 	came
back 	with 	this message scrawled across •
the top - "this idea 	might 	work once a
cop;rwriter 	gets ahold 	of it. 	R.A.V."
I guess 	I'm 	pretty persistent, plus I 	had 	a
patient supervisor. 	He 	made 	me 	keep
rewriting 	and 	rewriting 	until 	I 	got 	it right.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 141 of 253 --

I 	had 	not 	been 	in 	direct 	mail 	for 	even 	a
year 	when 	I 	was 	assigned 	to 	write 	a 	grass-
roots 	fundraising 	appeal 	for 	a 	tax 	reform
client. 	This 	assign1nent 	changed 	my 	life.
Initial 	test 	results 	were 	off the 	charts 	and
'the 	second 	wave 	of tests 	worked 	to
nearly 	every 	list 	we 	tried. 	Within 	18
months, 	this 	package 	was 	making 	news
not 	just 	in 	d.m. circles, 	but 	in 	the
mainstream 	business 	media.
The Wall Street Journal 	reported 	actual
results 	on 	page 	one 	- 	"13 	million 	mailed,
300,000 	favorable 	replies."
FORTUNE 	reported 	it 	as 	one 	of 	the 	most
successful 	political 	fundraising 	appeals
ever 	mailed. 	And 	I 	lmew 	what 	I 	wanted
to 	do 	for 	the 	rest 	of my 	life - 	write
•
blockbuster 	direct response.
The 	Graduate 	School 	of
Direct 	Marketing
My 	next 	opportunity 	to 	create 	break-
through 	results 	was 	as 	copy 	chief for
Rodale 	Press 	(now 	Rodale, 	Inc.), 	the
publisher 	dubbed 	by 	Denny 	Hatch 	as
"the 	grad 	school 	of direct 	marketing."
At 	Rodale 	I was 	surrounded 	by 	some 	of
the 	top 	talent 	in 	direct 	marketing,
including 	half 	a 	dozen 	individuals 	now
enshrined 	in 	the 	Direct 	Marketing
Association's 	Circulation 	Hall 	of Fame.
Again 	I 	had 	great 	teachers 	and 	ended 	up
\Vfiting 	direct 	mail 	controls 	for 	most 	of
Rodale's 	titles 	including 	Prevention,
American Woodworker, Scuba Diving,
Bicycling 	and 	The New Fann.
Two 	of 	these campaigns 	won 	response-
based 	Echo 	Awards 	from 	the 	DMA; 	one
sold 	over 	a 	million 	paid 	subscriptions.
As 	copy 	chief, I 	reviewed 	all 	copy 	from
freelance writers including 	one 	of the
most 	important 	figures 	in 	the 	company's
success, 	legendary 	copywriter, 	publisher
and 	direct 	marketer, 	Eugene 	Schwartz.
Gene 	was 	not 	only 	my 	mentor 	but 	one 	of
my 	frrst clients when I 	hung 	out 	my
shingle 	as a freelancer. I consider 	being
paid 	to 	write 	for 	Gene 	as the highest
recognition 	I've 	received in 	my 	career.
Becoming 	the 	go ... to copywriter
for 	$6.9 	billion 	institution
Over 	a 	dozen 	years ago I 	was 	hired to
write 	a 	newsletter 	subscription promotion
for one 	of the largest 	and 	best-1m own
medical 	institutions 	in 	the 	world. I 	can't
mention 	their 	name 	in a directory 	of
"junk 	mail" writers, 	but 	I will send 	you 	a
sample 	of this mega-control 	in 	exchange
for a 	sample 	of 	yours.
This 	package 	has 	surpassed 	not 	only
everything 	I've 	ever done, 	but 	possibly
every 	promotion 	ever 	created 	in 	the
newsletter, circulation 	and 	health
information 	industries. 	With 	regular
updates, the 	package 	has 	rolled 	out 	in
multi-million 	piece 	quantities for 	over 	50
consecutive quarters. 	It's 	pulled 	over
3.6 	million 	paid 	orders 	and 	$80 	million
in 	initial subscription 	revenue 	to date.
Response- 	it's 	still 	the 	only 	thing
I 	am 	all about results. 	I've 	had 	big 	oppor-
tunities 	with 	big 	marketers, 	and 	I've
produced 	copy 	that has 	made 	my 	clients
very 	successful 	in 	the 	mail and 	online.
For 	27 	years, 	my 	copy 	has sold 	not 	only
books, 	magazines 	and 	newsletters, 	but
mortgages, 	vacuum 	cleaners, 	gourmet
foods 	and 	IPY 	even 	my 	own 	self; I 	was
elected 	to public office, finishing first 	in
a four-way race, after writing a letter 	that
con1mitted 	my 	campaign promises to
black 	and white for all to read.
I 	am 	driven to 	sell. 	I'll 	swap 	my 	samples
for 	yours. 	Then 	let's 	talk 	about 	results. 	e!) _
P .S. 	Ask 	about 	my 	magazine renewal copy
that 	beat 	the control 	by 	a 	"stellar" 	40-50%.
Mark 	Everett 	Johnson 	may be reached
at 603-465-3888 	or 	mark@copy.pro.

Vibrant 	Edge 	Copywriting
Sales Writing That Gets The Response
Cheryl 	Malcham 	·•
Freelance Copywriter
Looking 	for 	a Experienced 	Copywriter 	who 	really understands
the 	Alternative 	Health 	and 	Self 	Help 	Direct 	Response 	Industries?
One 	who 	can 	truly 	get 	you 	the 	response 	you 	desire?
Look 	no 	further.
I 	can 	provide 	you 	with 	vibrant, 	comprehensive, 	and 	factually 	correct copy.
As 	you 	know, 	writing 	for 	the 	Health and 	Self Help markets is 	not 	a 	hit-or..:miss
proposition. You need accurate 	copy. 	You need a 	copywriter 	who 	understands
what 	he 	or 	she 	is writing 	about 	and can convey 	the 	value 	to 	the 	prospect.
Obviously, 	you need 	someone 	who 	comprehends 	FDA 	and 	FTC 	Guidelines and
Scientific Research - 	to 	make 	your 	job 	easier and 	less 	stressful! 	Someone 	who
gets 	it 	right 	the 	first 	time ... to 	meet 	the 	deadline.
And 	you 	need 	the 	passion. 	Someone 	who 	is 	deeply 	excited 	about 	your products!
Why 	am 	I 	the 	right 	Copywriter 	for 	your 	marteting 	needs?
• 	years 	of Sa 	and 	Marketing Experience. 	I 	think 	like 	a
salesperson. 	I 	intuitively 	know 	what 	sells.
• 	Degree 	in 	Clinical 	Nutrition 	and a 	passion 	for 	Alternative
Health 	and 	Personal 	G 	give me 	the 	expertise to 	handle 	your 	most
complex 	topics.
• 	Experience 	in 	both 	Web 	and 	Direct 	Mail 	Copywriting
Landing pages, 	YICb 	sales 	letters, 	re-order letters, content-rich articles,
brochures -I've 	done 	them 	all.
Give 	me 	a 	call 	today.
1would 	love 	to 	discuss 	your 	project 	- 	what 	your 	needs 	are 	- 	and 	how 	I 	can 	apply
my 	talents and 	skills 	to 	assist 	you 	in 	achieving 	the 	response rate 	you 	deserve!

with 	asafdaaffadassadaadadadsadsaad (ADSAAFAFDAA). They're yours whether 	we

•
Sincerely,
Cheryl 	Malcham
Vibrant Edge Copywriting
PS: 	A 	little confession 	in 	advance: 	I'm 	NOT 	the most experienced copywriter on the 	·
planet. In fact, 	my 	freelancer career is 3 	years 	yom1g. 	However, longevity is 	no 	guarantee
at all 	of delivering successful outcomes 	in 	any particular 	situation. 	As 	a matter 	of fact, a
set 	of"fresh 	eyes" 	can 	be 	a big plus. For 	this 	reason, though, I give every client I accept a
sintple 	guarantee 	with 	every writing project: 	if results don't achieve the agreed upon
target, 	I'll 	re-write 	and keep working as long 	as 	the client 	is 	testing. 	I'm 	not going to
walk 	away, 	shrug 	my shoulders and mumble "win some, lose some" 	-like 	lawyers do
after their clients are 	handcuffed 	and led 	away 	to the gallows! But 	we're 	ahead 	of
ourselves, aren't we? You can't make a decision to retain me now, and I can't make the
decision to accept you now either. Let's 	explore.
PPS: 	The legendary economist 	Harry 	Dent 	Jr. 	says: 	demographics 	are 	destiny. 	The
peaking 	of the boomer population, and the older generation immediately ahead 	of it,
represents an 	unprecedented 	wave 	of opportunity 	for astute marketers 	in 	the alternative
health 	field·. 	Further, to 	your 	benefit, there is a 	vezy 	positive psychological factor:
resistance to aging. But, there is also more competition and clutter than ever. The
inteJ:net, 	with 	all 	its 	positives, 	has 	virtually 	erased 	barrier 	to entry. Also, in part because
of all 	this 	clutter 	and 	''noise", 	consumers are more skeptical than ever. Navigating these
waters, tricky. But the time for aggressiveness, for growth, for expansion 	IS 	now. 	You
need the best tearn around you, to fully exploit this 	titne .

How 	oftet1 	do 	you 	~Mutter 	- 	or 	yell 	-
«~ 	N
•••	•• 	•
Dear
As 	you 	can 	see, 	I've 	sent 	you 	a 	gift. 	Push 	the 	button.
The 	lion 	will 	roar 	for 	you 	- 	I'M 	SURROUNDED 	BY 	IDIOTS! 	You
can 	save 	your 	voice!
Enclosed 	is 	a 	duplicate 	copy 	of 	a 	letter 	of 	introduction 	I
sent 	you 	about 	a 	week 	ago. 	It 	lays 	out 	my 	case 	for 	myself 	as 	a
copywriter 	who 	may 	be 	a 	perfect 	fit 	with 	and 	a 	valuable 	asset 	for
your 	business. 	It 	offers 	a 	no-fee 	get-acquainted 	consultation,
and 	two 	Special 	Reports 	as 	a 	thank 	you 	gift 	just 	for 	having 	that
exploratory 	discussion 	with 	me. 	Yet, 	I 	have 	not 	heard 	from 	you. 	I
wonder: 	why?
Maybe 	you 	are 	completely, 	thoroughly, 	orgasmically 	happy
with 	all 	the 	results 	of 	your 	advertising 	and 	marketing, 	and 	the
people 	you 	rely 	on 	to 	support 	it 	all 	with 	great 	copy. 	If 	so 	- 	I
can 	only 	say: 	congratulations. 	But 	it's 	more 	likely 	you've 	just
been 	too_busy 	to 	give 	time 	to 	considering 	what 	I 	might
contribute. 	Maybe 	you're 	a 	skeptic 	- 	even 	a 	cynic 	- 	about
freelance 	copywriters. 	But, 	ARE 	YOU 	SURE 	you 	want 	to 	continue
getting 	your 	marketing 	done 	as 	you've 	been 	getting 	it 	done?
Please 	re-consider 	the 	enclosed.
Sincerely,
Cheryl 	Malcham

table than eaten.)

Introduction
n is 	what 	vou 	d on ...
and 	t 	is 	where 	uou 	11 	find 	it!
Congratulations on 	your 	purchase 	of the only published source 	of in-depth
inforrnation 	on 	the top direct 1nailers 	in 	the 	Unit~d 	States.
Millions 	of direct mail pieces pass through 	the 	postal system 	every day. 	Yet
the only 	record 	of this activity is the database maintained by our archive
staff. 	Our 	archivists 	sort 	through thousands 	of packages each month and
catalog 	and 	code 	them. This information is 	then 	offered to you 	in 	the only
direct 	mail 	reference 	of its 	kind 	the 	2004 	Directory 	of Major Mailers 	&
What 	They 	Mail.
With hundreds 	of new 	volume mailers never before listed and exclusive
infox rnation 	on 	thousands 	more 	you'll 	see 	just 	who 	the direct mailers 	in
the country are and 	what 	they are mailing 	to 	help 	you 	find prospects for 	your
products 	and 	services 	or 	to determine the best strategies for your 	own 	direct
mail 	promotions.
To 	help 	you 	find 	the companies you 	need 	quickly, 	the 	2004 	Directory 	of
Major Mailers 	has 	been 	indexed 	in 	a 	number 	of ways 	use 	the 	Category
Index 	to 	look up 	a 	mailer 	by 	product 	or 	industry... the 	Format Index 	to 	find
mailers 	who 	purchase a specific product 	or 	service... the 	Marketing 	Index, 	if
you need 	to 	reach 	companies 	that 	use telemarketing, direct response methods,
Internet advertising 	or 	international marketing... or 	the 	Geographical Index
to 	find 	a particular mailer 	by 	city 	or 	state.
Access to 	the Archive
Don't 	forget! 	As 	a subscriber, 	you 	have priority access 	to 	the Who's Mailing
What Archive 	the world's largest library 	of direct mail, 	with 	more 	than
136,000 	samples 	of consumer, business, fundraising, and catalog mailings. 	If
you 	want 	to 	see 	the 	actual mailing package from any 	of the 	major mailers
listed 	in 	this directory, please call 	(215) 238-5225 	or 	fax 	your request to
(215) 238-5350. 	An 	archivist 	will 	assist 	in 	helping 	you 	find 	what 	you 	need,
as well as tell you the costs involved.
The 	Directory 	of Major Mailers 	is designed 	to 	provide the detailed inforrna-
tion 	you 	need 	to 	make 	your 	business grow. Please 	don't 	hesitate 	to contact 	us
with 	any 	questions, ideas 	or 	suggestions so next year's edition 	will 	be 	even
more valuable. 	We 	look fonvard to hearing from you!
Ellen 	K.. 	Donnelly, 	Editor
Directory 	of Major Mailers
Tele: 	(215)238-54 75
edonnelly@napco.com

ww 	. 	aior 	ailers.com

Introducing 	a 	NEW 	Ho 	· 	e Service
from 	the 	Directory 	of 	Major 	Mailers
The 	Directory 	of Major Mailers 	(DMM) 	Hotline 	is 	a 	new
service 	from 	the 	editors 	of Directory 	of Major 	Mailers 	that
provides 	quarterly 	u 	of .	NEW 	major 	mailers 	received 	in
our 	archives. 	The 	DMM 	Hotline 	contains 	approximately 	50-100
NEW 	major 	mailers 	each 	quarter 	with 	details 	such 	as 	mailer
name, 	address, 	phone, 	fax, 	Web 	address 	and 	the 	direct 	mail
contact. 	Plus, 	you'll 	have 	detailed 	summary infonnation 	about
each 	company's 	mailing 	activity ... you '11 	see 	when 	each 	mailing
dropped ... what 	exactly was 	offered ... what 	fonnat 	was
used ... and 	more!
These 	are 	your prime 	prospects 	for 	new 	business 	pitches.
Don't 	let 	hundreds 	of valuable 	NEW 	sales 	prospects 	-
pre-qualified 	users 	of 	all 	kinds 	of direct 	marketing 	senices
and 	supplies 	slip 	away 	to 	your 	competitors .
The 	Directory 	of 	Major 	Mailers 	Hotline 	Senice 	is
delivered 	on 	a quarterly 	basis 	(January, 	April, July, 	October).
You 	will 	receive 	an 	e-mail 	each 	quarter with 	a link 	to
an 	Excel 	file 	you 	can 	download. 	A one-year 	subscription
is 	only 	$199f
Order 	the 	Directory 	of Major 	Mailers 	Hotline 	Senice
in 	combination 	"With 	the 	2004 	Directory of Major 	Mailers
print 	edition 	or 	CD-ROM 	and 	pay 	only 	$149 	for 	the
.l:l....~iy 	Mt.

-- 165 of 253 --

*Why 	OPC 	is 	a bargain
1 : 	Money 	paid 	to 	Host 	is less, 	same 	or 	only a 	bit 	more than costs 	of
securing 	clients 	via 	other 	means
•
2: 	Clients 	may 	be 	of 	higher 	quality, 	if 	from a quality Host, who
conditions 	and 	trains 	them 	well
3: 	Host's 	endorsement 	and 	platforms give 	you 	Authority with
clients
4: 	Speed: 	can 	obtain clients 	in 	clump(s)
5: Continuation: system 	put 	on 	auto-pilot, 	so 	you 	re-capture time
from 	seeking 	clients 	to 	invest elsewhere
6: Leverage: success with 	one 	facilitates another 	such 	relationship
-

-- 168 of 253 --

TllANSITION
MILK 	COW 	ACCO
•
Many consultants 	I 	copywriters replace the set
income and 'security' 	of job and paycheck with one
or two 	of 	two things.
One 	is 	the 	transition 	market 	- sort 	of 	like the rebound
relationship; 	not 	intended to last. 	The 	transition market or short-
term market is a 	'place' 	where 	you 	can get jobs rather easily in 	part
because 	of 	low pay, but get many, and grind out the rent and the
light bill. 	A 	factory copywriting 	approach to a 	niche 	market 	is 	one
good 	example 	of 	this.
The 	other is 	the 	milk 	cow 	account- 	a retainer 	client for whom a
loosely 	agreed 	upon 	variety 	of 	work is to 	be 	done, some pre-
determined, 	some 	as 	needed, 	on 	call like a volunteer
frreman 	.... adding 	up 	to a 	lot more 	work 	for 	less 	money than 	any
other 	work 	or client 	relationship. 	Compensation may include cash
and barter, such as office space, secretarial support, aggressive
center-of-influence activity on 	your 	behalf, 	free 	ad 	space 	in 	a
media, etc. 	You 	should get 	paid 	every 2 weeks, 	to 	avoid a
situation 	of 	the client "getting into 	you" 	for money, and, ideally,
get some pre-payment 	on 	the entire contract. 	You 	need 	not
necessarily forego royalties or other upside in such a situation.
lj
\ 	Ia
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

Agencies

Halbert
Carlton
LOTS 	of journeyman 	do 	it- 	rough drafts for other copywriters

Client 	protection 	and 	confidentiality, 	in 	writing

need 	solo 	pkgs., 	risk 	losing 	control 	of 	client
if 	can't 	provide
Speed

•
hat 	Do 	You 	Do?•
''Have 	you 	ever driven through a 	neighborhood 	in 	the 	evening
and 	seen a 	home 	so 	beautifully 	and 	magnificently 	lit, 	so
perfectly landscaped ... the paint 	on 	the 	home 	flawless, 	the
colors 	distinctive 	and 	textured, the 	flowers 	blooming, 	the 	lawn,
a sea 	of 	green .... a 	home 	so 	impressive 	you 	had· to 	circle 	back,
pull 	over, 	stop, 	get 	out 	of 	your 	car 	and 	admire 	it? 	That's 	what
I 	do. 	I 	make 	people's 	homes 	into 	works 	of 	art."
"Have 	you 	ever seen 	someone 	with ... 	or 	been 	to 	someone's
home 	and 	seen that they 	have ... 	a 	dog 	so 	well-behaved, ·so
obedient, 	so 	civilized, 	yet still 	happy, 	interested, 	friendly ... that
you 	wished 	yours 	was 	like 	that 	dog? 	That's 	what 	I 	do .... "
From Joel Bauer \
•

~hat 	Do You 	Do?
"I'm a 	direct-response 	specialist. 	That 	means 	when 	I 	write 	a
sales 	letter 	for 	your 	business, 	I'm 	going 	to 	do 	it 	in 	such 	a 	way
that 	the 	reader 	can't 	put 	it 	down 	until 	he 	reaches 	your
signature 	and 	the 	last 	P.S. 	And 	then he's 	not 	going 	to 	be 	able
to 	resist 	responding 	to 	whatever 	you 	are 	asking 	him 	to 	do 	-
presumably, 	call, 	come 	in, 	buy. 	There 	are 	five 	major•
determining 	factors 	to 	success 	with 	a 	marketing 	campaign 	in
general 	and 	a 	sales 	letter, 	specifically, 	and 	drawing 	on 	all 	my
experience, 	I'm 	going 	to 	align 	those 	five 	th·· 	~ .gs 	in 	your 	favor."
Jerry Buchanan

wtll 	never 	be 	an 	advertising 	victim 	again."

chooses, 	anywhere 	in 	the 	country 	he 	chooses- 	working 	from 	his 	motor-home

Within 	2 	to 	3 	weeks, 	there's 	money.

cessful. 	or 	even 	how 	to make 	a living

The best way to map out your

·which of those potential mentors
is 	the 	right one for you? Here's 	a
brief guide to picking a guru that 	I
might 	do 	you 	some good:
••
1. Rlltl 	saueone 	you like
If you~ re going 	to 	be 	follow-
ing someone 's 	advice, 	get some-
one whose advice you can stand
to 	bear. If your chosen mentor is
too cranky, 	too jumpy or too eso-
teric for 	you 	to 	bear, 	look for 	someone 	else to
be 	your main 	guide.
2. Use 	your 	sense
If a particular guru's wisdom doesn ' t 	seem to
make sense to 	you, 	ask for 	details.
If the background philosophy behind any one
genius's program for success rings false, then
select another genius. A 	guy 	with 	nothing 	but
one swell idea 	is 	great 	as a source for 	that 	one
idea- 	but 	riot 	as your general guide 	to 	market-
ing and management

not 	tle.

Dan Kennedy
'
uring 	a 	break 	at 	one 	of 	my
seminars, 	a· young 	doctor
and 	his 	wife 	presented 	me
. 	with 	this 	problem:
"We don't have much 	money to ad-
vertise 	or 	promote, 	and 	we 	know
next 	to 	nothing 	about 	doing 	that 	Of
all 	the 	things 	you 	teach, 	what 	is 	the
one 	thing 	we 	might 	do, 	that 	doesn •t
~ 	mpch 	man~ 	to 	get a lot of new
patients?"
Their 	question 	controlled 	the 	sec-
ond 	half 	of 	that 	day's 	speech, 	and
here are the key ideas I 	talked 	about-
the 	five 	big 	realizatio~ 	that 	lead to
lots 	of.new patients:•
1. 	There Is No Answer
rve met a 	lot of veiy successfulchi-
ropradors, 	and 	every 	time 	I discuss
marketing 	with 	them-	, 	I'm 	sti uck 	by
the 	diversity 	of 	the 	things 	they 	do 	to
•	•
32 •
•
(
•
• 	• es 	o
•
•
•
•
•
create 	new 	patient flow. 	I 	summarize
this in seminars by saying: 	"Diversity
is 	the 	creative opposite 	of 	laziness."
Years 	back, 	a 	very 	successful 	book
was 	titled 	The 	Lazy 	Man's 	\t\by 	to
Riches, 	~d you 	can 	clearly see 	why
it 	was 	so successful - 	everybody
dream~ 	of getting rich without effort,
thin without sacrifice. But the truth is
that the 	lazy doctor's 	way to 	new pa·
tient 	flow 	just 	doesn't 	exist 	In 	fact,
there is no one big secret to getting 20, 	.
30, 	50, 	or 	100 	new 	patients 	this
month. 	Don't 	hunt 	for 	it. 	You 	won't
find 	it. 	It 	doesn't 	exist. 	But 	there are
30, 	50, 	70, 	even 100 	different ways to• 	•
get 	a 	new 	patient, and the more of
those 	you 	put 	to 	work 	simultane-
ously 	on 	your 	behalf, 	the 	better off
you'll be.

The 	smart 	doctor 	does everything he
can possibly 	think of 	to 	facilitate 	this

practice 	depends 	more 	on 	its 	referral
rate 	than 	on 	any 	other single statistic
you 	might monito:r: •
3. 	Educate, Educate, Educate
I 	like 	to 	have 	doctors 	memorize
this 	statement: The better infonned-
but never bored- a 	patient is, the bet-
ter. his 	follow-through 	and 	success,
and 	the 	more refen-als he'll generate.
Every 	day 	people 	flow 	through
your 	office 	who 	in 	20, 	30, 	even 	40
years 	of life, 	through 	high 	school.
through 	college, 	on 	the 	job, 	even 1 JD-
der 	treatment 	by 	other 	doctors, 	have
never 	had 	anybody 	make 	them 	un-
derstand what 	their body 	is 	made 	of,
how 	it 	works, 	whv 	it 	malfunctions,•
and 	what 	they 	can 	do 	to 	help 	them-•
selves 	get 	well 	and 	stay well.
It 	is 	my 	absolute 	conviction 	that
those 	doctors 	who 	deliver 	the 	best,
most 	thorou~ 	most 	dynamic 	pa-
tient 	education 	programs 	have 	the
highest 	referral 	numbers. 	The 	corre-
lation is clear-and predictable.
4. 	Excellence Produces Excellent
Results
Walt 	Disney 	advised, 	"Find 	ways
to do what you do 	so well that people
can't 	resist 	telling 	others 	about 	you."
H you 	want 	an 	excellent 	income, 	be.
ing good 	just isn't good 	enough.
Many 	doctors 	are 	puzzled 	and be-
fuddled 	when 	I 	ask 	them 	to 	des< Jibe
their 	"unique 	selling proposition."
That 	is 	the 	succinct, 	compelling 	an-
swer 	to 	tllls 	tough 	que~on: 	"vr.hy
should 	I, 	your 	prospective 	patient,
choose 	you 	versus 	any 	other 	doctor?
Why you versus all the 	other 	doctors
in 	the 	Yellow Pages?" You 	see, 	you
need a 	package 	or a collection of "ex-
cellence 	factors" 	to 	wan-ant 	being 	a
prospective patient's 	No. 	1 	choice.
_Also, 	the 	newest, 	hottest 	market-
ing concept of the 1990s is the 	"value
added 	selling 	proposition." 	This 	an-
swers: "What do vou 	do for me that is•
an 	unexpected, 	abnoz n1al, 	pleasant
•
-
GUEST EDITORIAL
surprise?" 	I 	love 	to 	see chiropractors
with 	lending 	libraries 	of 	self-
improvement 	and 	health-related
books 	and 	tapes 	for 	their 	patients;
fresh 	fruit 	and 	juice 	in 	the 	reception
area; good, 	helpful 	products 	availa-
ble -like 	low back supports and 	pil-
lows; 	and 	all 	sorts of other "extras." 	I
know 	their 	patients 	go 	to 	their 	next-
doorneighoors, their co-workers. and
their friends 	and 	say, 	"Hey, 	you 	can't
believe 	what's 	going 	on 	at 	my 	doc-
tor's office" 	or, 	uLet me tell 	you about
my doctor!"
5. 	Think-AndGrowRich
I've been 	privileged 	to 	work 	on· a
sequence 	of 	three 	made-for-tele-
vision 	programs 	presenting 	audio-
tape 	courses 	based 	on 	the 	famous
book 	by 	Napoleon 	Hill, 	Think 	and
Grow 	llich. I've also been 	privy to 	in-
. terviews with hugely successful peo-
ple like Tom 	Monaghan 	of 	Domino 's
Pizza, 	W. 	Clement 	Stone, 	"Famous
Amos," Walter Annenberg , and many
others 	who 	uedit 	their 	successes 	to
the 	essence 	of Hill's philosophy: 	To
grow 	rich, 	we 	must 	think rich, 	o~ 	as
Earl Nightingale 	put 	it, 	"We 	become
what we 	think 	about 	most, 	I 	think
this 	applies 	on 	three 	different levels
- 	to the issue of increasing new patient
flow. •
Fiist, the creative invention and in·
novation process. I 	once 	asked 	the
chief executive of a fairly large but se-
verely 	t:Iuubled corporation 	who 	he
had working on a particular problem.
"Nobody," 	he 	replied 	dourly. 	When 	I
asked 	'vhy, he 	said, 	"Because there is
no 	solution.n 	You see, 	you 	have 	to
"work on" 	ways 	to 	enhance 	your 	ex-
cellence, 	better 	educate 	your 	pa-•
tients, 	and otherwise inspire referrals
and 	attract 	new 	patients 	every 	day.
That has 	to 	be 	a 	priority 	in 	your
thinking. 	H you 	don't think about it a
lot, 	in 	a positive, 	constructive 	man-
ner, 	nothing will change.
Second~ 	the 	mission. 	H 	you 	find
tWo 	chiropractors of 	similar 	experi-
ence 	and skill located near each other
in 	the 	same 	city 	and 	one 	is 	thriving
•
•
while 	the 	other is struggling, 	you'll
discover 	that 	the 	two 	doctors 	think
differently 	about their 	missions. 	The
thriving 	doctor 	will 	exhibit a 	mis-
sionarj, 	evangelistic 	zeal 	for 	helping•
people 	get 	and 	stay 	healthy. 	The
•
struggling doctor 	will 	be 	working for
his 	checkbook only.
When we 	are 	"turned 	on" by 	what
we do 	for 	its 	own 	merits, 	that sincere
enthusiasm somehow - 	don't ask me
to explain 	how! 	-acts as a 	magnet 	to
attract the 	opportunities, people, 	and
other 	resources 	we 	need 	to 	be
successful. 	·
Third 	is 	what 	I 	call 	patient 	per-•
spective, 	or 	thinking 	like the 	patient
thinks. 	It's 	empathy 	and 	more. You
have 	to 	continually 	remind 	yourself
of 	what 	it's like 	to 	be 	injured 	or 	in
pain, 	confused, 	worried, 	intimi-
dated, 	unsure 	of 	who 	to 	trust. 	Think
like 	your 	prospective · patients 	and
new 	patients 	think 	and 	you ' ll 	be
much more 	effective at securing their
trust and 	cooperation.
The synthesis 	of 	all 	this 	is 	that the
nature 	and 	quality 	of 	your 	thinking
have 	enormous 	impact 	on 	your 	new
patient flow.
•
•
Dan 	S. 	Kennedy 	is 	a
popular 	speaker 	and
seminar leadei; the au~
thor 	of 	numerous
books 	and 	audio-
cassette 	programs,
and the editor 	of 	The
Ptoctice.Building 	Se-
crets 	letter. 	He 	is the former owner of an
advertising 	agency 	ai;ld 	for 	the 	last 	ten
years has been a leading consultant in the
direct 	marketing 	industry. 	His 	most · re-
cent 	book, 	The Ultimate 	Sales 	Letter, 	is
now 	in bookstores nationwide. 	MI: 	Ken-
nedy 	is 	a 	repeat 	guest 	lecturer at 	Parke~
state associations, 	and 	several 	different
practice management companies, and the
Success1rak 	Division 	of 	his 	publishing
company 	has 	conducted over 	200 	semi-
nars 	for 	chiropractors 	and 	dentists
throughout the 	U.S . and Canada. 	Free 	in-
fomlatian about 	his.materials 	for 	chi.ro-
practors 	is 	available 	on 	request 	5818 N .

-- 182 of 253 --

hen 	you 	think "direct
marketing," 	you 	do 	not
usually 	think 	II chiropractic."
Yet, 	you 	can 	do direct marketing for non-
direct 	.marketing 	businesses, 	even 	your
ch iropractic practice.
The 	secret: Follow 	10 	rules.
Rule 	1: 	Always 	make 	an 	<?ffer 	(or 	off e rs). 	Direct
rnarkenng 	ts 	selling 	in 	pnnt 	selling 	with 	medta. 	Your
task 	IS 	to 	make 	a 	dl!ect-response 	offer. 	The 	more
mteresting 	and 	appealmg the offer, 	the 	better. But 	the
b 	. 	N 	d 	" 	"	most 	Important 	asLc 	po1nt 	rs: 	ever 	en 	a 	conversatton
in 	CL."1Y 	med1a without making a 	direct 	offer.
Ch eck 	your 	yellow pages ad. 	Odds 	are, no 	offer. 	A lot 	of
bus1 ne sses 	even 	run 	newspaper 	ads 	with no offers. 	They
have 	what's 	ca lled 	an 	rmpbed 	offer. 	A lot of businesses run
this 	kind 	of ad, 	whtch 	essentially 	says, 	"Here 	we 	are; 	here's
wh at 	we 	do.''
Rule 	2: 	Give 	a reason 	to 	respond 	right 	now . 	Your
offer mu st 	force 	and 	compel 	your potential 	pat tent 	to
move now. 	You r 	goal 1	s 1mmediare response. A 	pla1n
vantlla 	offer 	won't do 	Jt.
Rule 	3: 	Give 	dear 	instructions. 	When 	you 	put
toge~ h e r 	any 	markettng 	tool 	ad, 	flyer, 	sales letter, Web
site, phone 	script, etc. 	· 	it should 	be 	a 	single, 	focused
path 	that: 	leads 	the 	prospective 	panent 	to 	a 	point 	of
dectsion 	and action. 	At 	that 	potnt, you 	musr. 	tell 	the
prospect exactly what 	you 	want 	him 	to 	do 	next1 	how and
when, 	and 	what wtH 	happen 	as 	soon 	as 	he 	does.
Stop 	sending 	out anyth1ng absent 	of such 	dear
•
tnstructtons.
Rule 	4: Track a nd m ea sure . 	You 	need real, 	hard 	facts
and data to make 	good, 	intelligent marketing decisions.
Tracking 	means accurately collecting 	all 	the infom1ation
you 	need 	to 	detennme 	what 	advertts1ng is 	working and
what 	1sn't, 	which 	offer 	is 	pulling 	and 	wh1ch 	isn't, 	what
marketing has 	tracoon 	and what doesn'r.. 	Tracking 	leads 	to
knowing what your 	return 	on 	Investment is 	for 	each 	dollar.
Set 	up systems 	to 	capture the 	data 	you 	need. 	Set 	aside
arne 	to 	do 	the necessary 	analys1s. 	If It's 	painful 	and
confusing 	at 	first, 	1t'll 	get 	eas1er. 	And 	tt 	will 	prove profitable.
From now on, do 	not 	spend 	any 	marketing dollars
without tracking 	the 	ROI.
Rule 5: Brand a s a by- product. 	Traditional 	brand ..
building 	ts 	fine 	for 	g1ant 	companies 	with 	huge 	budgets
vytng 	for store shelf space and consumers' recogniuon. 	If
· 	you are the 	CEO 	of 	Hetnz 	or Coors, 	playing 	w1th 	other
peoples' 	n1arbles, 	by 	all means 	buy 	brand identity.
But 	if you 	are an encrepreneur investing 	you r own marbles,
then 	focus 	on response and 	sales. 	lf you 	devetop 	brand

6 8 	C H I R O P RACTIC 	ECCNOM :CS
recognition 	accident-ally 	along· the
way, 	great. But 	do 	not 	sp end
exclusively 	on 	creating 	it.
Brand .. b uil ding 	is 	for 	patient
people 	with 	very 	deep 	pockets.
That's 	probably 	not 	you.
Rule 	6: 	Follow 	up. 	If a 	person
reads 	your .ad, 	calls your 	clinic, 	and 	.
asks 	a· question, 	make 	sure 	the 	CA
does 	something 	wi th 	that 	opportunity.
The 	CA 	should 	capture 	the 	person's
name, 	address, 	e .. mail, 	and 	offer to
sch~dule 	a consultation 	if that 	is
what 	yow; 	offer was.
An other 	follow .. up 	opportunity:
Leads 	from 	scr eenings 	or 	refe rr als.
Make 	calls 	and 	p ut 	those names 	on
your 	newsletter mailing 	list.
Plan 	a sequence 	of 	things 	to 	be
done 	to 	follow 	up.
Rule 	7: 	Use 	strong 	C<?PY· 	Sales•
and 	subtlety 	rarely 	go 	hand 	in hand.
There 	is 	enormous, 	overwhelming
competi t ion 	for 	attention 	and 	clutter
you 	must 	cut 	through. 	Use 	compelling
copy 	in 	your 	marketing 	materials.
Rule 	8: M imic 	mail-o rder
advertising. 	Any and all 	ads 	for 	any
business 	shou ld 	mimic 	mail.. order ads.
Accumulate a file of fuU .. page
· newspaper 	and magazine ads that 	are
mail--order 	ads 	that 	ask 	you to buy or
at 	least 	request 	free 	inforntation.
'Whenever 	you 	prepare 	or 	approve your
ads, 	fl yers, 	sales 	letters, or Web sites,
get 	out 	this 	file 	and 	compare. 	Yours
should look 	like 	the 	ones 	in 	the 	file.
Rule 	9: 	Results rule. Period.
. 	Opinions 	don't 	count; 	only results .
If an 	ad 	br ings 	in 	patient s, 	it 	is good.
If 	it 	doesn't, 	it's 	no t.
Rule 	10: 	Get 	tough; become
disciplined. 	And 	go 	on 	a 	direct--
mar keting diet 	fo r six months.
Whe n you go 	o n 	a diet, 	yow
• 	Purge 	your refr igerator 	and
cup board s 	of 	j unk 	foods. 	And
keep 	them 	fr ee 	of th em .
• 	Decide 	on 	an 	ea t ing 	plan 	and
stick to 	it 	pat i ently a nd
persistently.
• 	Get 	some 	coo ls , 	such 	as a 	scale.
• 	Count 	som eth in g 	c alo ries, 	fat
gr ams, 	or 	We ight 	W a tch e r
WWW.CH I ROECO.COM
•
po i nts 	so you can subtract 	by
the 	nu mbers 	and 	manage 	with
numbers.
• 	Seep 	up 	yo ur exercise.
Use 	the 	same 	steps 	with 	your
transfer rnation 	to 	become 	a 	lean,
mean 	di r ect 	markete r.
• 	Purge 	your 	business 	of ;.unk -
pretty 	brochures 	that 	violate 	rnost
or all of the 	above rules, 	dead 	ads
•that 	just 	lay 	t{lere, 	and gentle,
subtle, 	boring sal es 	letters. 	Out
with 	the 	old, 	in with 	the 	n ew.
• 	Decide 	on 	a 	marketing 	plan.
• 	Get 	some 	tools, 	such 	as 	new ads,
flyers, 	co~pons, 	sales 	letters,
Web 	sites, 	and 	scrip ts 	fo r
han dling 	incoming calls.
• 	Count. 	See 	Rule 	4: 	Track 	and
measure.
• 	Mentally 	exercise. 	Start 	rea~ling
books and articles, 	listening 	to
recordings, 	and 	going 	to
•
seminars 	about 	direct 	marketing .
You 	need 	to 	be 	very 	careful 	not 	to
let 	anything 	into 	your 	new direct
marketing 	world 	that 	doesn,t 	belong
there. 	.
Consider, 	for 	example, the bread
the 	restaurap.t 	gives me 	with 	my 	salad.
About 	once 	a 	week, I 	get 	a· Caesar·
salad 	with 	grilled 	chicken 	from a
nearby restaurant. It comes 	with 	a
small 	loaf of fresh ..baked 	bread.
When I 	get 	home, 	I 	throw 	th e
bread 	in to 	the 	garbage 	can 	in 	the
garage before 	ente r ing 	the 	h~:>Use.
Why? 	Because I 	don't 	have 	as 	much
disciplin e 	as 	I 	would 	like. 	If that bread
gets 	past 	the 	perimeter 	into 	my 	h ouse,
I' ll 	eat 	it. 	So 	I 	can 't 	let 	it 	in .
You've 	got 	to 	do 	the 	same 	t hing
with 	your 	practice. 	Anyth ing 	th at
doesn,t 	confonct 	to 	the prior 	10 	r ules ,
do 	not 	let 	it 	in 	at 	all. 	Just 	say 	no. 	~

I sued 	my 	daughter's school for conduct- 	ity 	child in 	my 	daughter's situation would 	or distributed 	in 	print, 	electronic 	or 	other 	forms .
.ve·
USA 	TODAY's 	weekly 	Ad 	Track 	fea-
ture 	sim.ply 	perpetuates 	costly 	miscon-
ceptions about advertising.
Writer Dottie 	Enrico 	says consumers
who 	participated 	in 	your 	poll 	of 	the 	new
Infiniti 	commercials did 	not 	particularly
like 	tl1e 	ads 	and 	didn't think 	they 	were
terribly 	effective 	("Sophisticated 	Infiniti
ads 	don't impress 	target 	viewers," 	Mon-
ey, 	Nov. 	25). 	And 	Infiniti's 	marlteting 	ex-
ecutive says 	an 	internal 	tracking 	survey
showed 	the 	new spot 	"was 	appreciated
for 	its 	novelty 	and 	entertainment value."
What 	Ad 	Track ignores, and the 	exec.. 	.
utive has lost 	sight 	of, 	is 	that 	the 	purpose
of 	advertising 	is not to 	be 	novel 	or 	to 	en-
tertain. 	The 	purpose of advertising 	is 	to
do 	one 	thing 	only 	get 	people 	to 	buy
the product 	or 	service advertised. 	·
There's 	a 	real 	danger 	in asking people
the wrong questions, which 	you 	do every
week 	in this feature. The last full-page
Quarterly Report Ad 	Track 	in Septem-
ber 	listed dozens 	of 	ads 	with 	the effec-
tiveness rating 	by 	.your 	consumer 	poll.
The 	ad 	rated 	highest 	by 	viewers 	was the
Everead 	Energizer 	bunny 	which 	45o/o
thou 	Wfti 	"very 	effective."
•
to 	sen p
Warner 	Bros .•
Bunny: 	Whose 	battery 	keeps 	it 	running?
•
We 	in 	direct-response 	marketing 	cir..
cles got a 	real 	hoot 	out of this~ 	·	we 	use the
Energizer 	bunny 	ad 	as 	an 	example of
what 	doesn't 	work. When speaking, 	we
ask 	audiences 	how 	many 	think 	the
brand 	name 	associated with the 	bunny 	is
.	,
ent
Energizer 	and how 	many 	think 	it's 	Dura-
cell. Even 	after 	the 	ads have 	run 	for
years, 	one third guess incorrectly.
The 	only 	vote that 	counts is 	the 	one
consumers 	make 	with 	their 	pocket-
books. 	Ads 	are 	not a popularity 	contest,
nor 	are 	they 	for 	"entertainment'' 	or
"novelty" 	value. People 	may 	find 	the 	En--
ergizer 	bunny 	amusing 	and 	entertaining,
but 	ii's 	insane for 	companies 	to 	spend
millions, 	even 	billions 	of 	dollarsJ 	promot-
ing products 	and 	services to 	deliver 	en-
tertainment value.
It's interesting to 	note 	that 	the 	Intiniti
Ad 	Track 	feature, 	in 	spiie 	of 	the 	fact
viewers 	neither 	particularly 	liked 	the 	ad
nor 	thought 	it 	was effective, 	says 	thai
sales 	of 	that 	particular 	1nodel 	of 	Infiniti
are 	uo 	8.1 ~~ 	since 	the 	new 	ad 	came 	out...
Stop asking 	the 	"Nrong 	questions 	and
perpetuating myths. 	Ask, 	"Did 	this 	ad-
vertisement 	or 	commercial 	make 	you 	go
out and 	buy 	the 	product?"
Pamela 	Yellen, 	CEO
Prospecting 	& 	Marketing 	Institute Inc.
Dan Kennedy, president 	·
Empire 	Communications
Phoenix, 	Ariz.'

responsible for 	my success,
wealth 	and sustained pre-

- Dan Kennedy
One 	inco1ne 	leap 	is fronz 	tdoer' 	to (marketer'. 	But 	is 	there an even more
significant, less understood (nearly 	secret), 	more advanced, next leap?
For 	the 	first 	time 	ever, 	I 	reveal 	the 	skill-
set, 	'science' 	and 	strategies 	more
responsible 	for 	my 	success, 	wealth 	and 	long,
sustained 	pre-eminence 	in 	my 	marketplace, 	and
invite 	you 	to 	adopt 	it 	as 	your 	own.
From 	Dan 	Kennedl'
Can 	you 	grasp the 	wisdom 	of 	mastery-level understanding 	and 	skill 	at
psychologically 	and 	emotionally 	bonding 	and 	connecting with 	your
customers, 	clients, 	patients, 	prospects 	or 	audience ..... at creating
relationship 	that transcends 	transactions .... at 	being an 	INFLUENTIAL 	voice
in 	their 	lives?
What 	we're 	about 	to discuss here 	should 	NOT 	be 	confused 	with
"copywriting" 	or (only) copy that sells.
There 	is 	another, distinctly different, I think fascinating kind 	of writing
that 	can 	be 	even 	more 	valuable to 	you- 	with its 	own 	psychology, strategies,
tactics. 	What! I@??! Something even more valuable than copy that sells. Yes
indeedy!
I call 	it: 	INFLUENTIAL 	Writing. •
There 	is a science 	and 	a set 	of 	skills for ....
Pu~ing 	your 	ideas 	~~ross 	with the written word

For 	15 years, I 	have 	written 	the 	monthly No 	B.S. 	Marketing Letter 	as well 	as
many 	other 	Vlritten-word 	works 	including several 	best-selling books. 	This 	is 	the
centrifugal 	force 	of 	an 	8- figure 	business 	empire. 	And 	you 	should 	not 	for 	a 	minute 	think
any 	of 	it 	occurs 	casually. 	There 	are 	formulas;. 	t:p.ere 	aJe 	ingredients 	that 	mqs.t. be 	present
in 	every 	written 	comm~gue; 	there 	is 	Vyry, 	very 	conscious, careful, deliberate strategy. 	It
may 	or 	may 	not 	surprise 	you 	that 	I 	am 	paid 7-figures 	for 	this 	contribution. 	Of 	course,
you 	may 	very 	well 	not 	be 	in 	a 	business 	that, 	on 	its surface, 	seems 	similar to Glazer-
Kennedy. 	But 	don't 	make 	the 	mistake 	of using 	that 	as 	justification for 	failing 	to 	bring 	the
power 	of 	INFLUENTIAL 	Writing 	to your 	business. 	EVERY 	business 	can 	have 	its
positioning, 	brand, 	and 	success 	enhanced 	by 	its 	owner/spokesperson 	becoming 	an
INFLUENTIAL 	Writer. 	In 	this 	letter, 	I'll 	tell 	you 	why. •
One 	time 	and 	one time only, 	I conducted a 	3-day 	training 	on 	INFLUENTIAL
Writing 	for an 	intimate 	group 	of20, 	who 	recognized 	its 	importance, and acknowledge
my 	mastery 	and 	scientific 	approach- 	so 	they 	each 	paid 	$10,000.00 	to 	be 	there. Included,
people 	you 	may 	know. 	Should 	know. 	Including:
John DuCane. CEO

INFLUENTIAL 	Writing
Examples 	employed 	for
commercial 	purposes ...
There 	are , of course , my weekly faxes 	and
my blog 	for GKIC 	Diamond Tele-Coaching
Members .... my 	Why People 	Fail
articles ... .Chapters 	1 of my book NO 	B.S.
RUTHLESS 	MANAGEMENT, 	pages 	218-219
and 	Chapter 	17 of NO 	B.S. 	BUSINESS
SUCCESS 	IN 	THE 	NEW ECONOMY and
much 	of my book, 	UNFINISHED 	BUStNESS.
Our GOLD 	Member Kathy Tolleson 	has done
a 	phenomenal job - 	one 	of the 	best 	I've yet
seen, 	maybe THE best - 	with 	a book 	tied 	to
her motorcyc les-for-women 	business 	(HEAR
ME 	ROAR: 	WOMEN, 	MOTORCYCLES AND
MENTAL 	HEALTH), 	and 	you 	can 	get an 	e-
book vers ion 	at RoarMotorcycles.com
A much more ponderous 	but very worthwhi le
read 	is the novel 	most recognized 	for be ing
INFLUENTIAL despite 	being 	a work of fiction,
Ayn 	Rand!s Atlas Shrugged. 	This 	book
established 	Ayn 	Rand 	as 	a sought 	after
lecturer, 	highly paid 	author of subsequent
works, 	and 	provided basis 	for developing an
organization 	of substantial 	size 	and
influence, 	as well 	as 	"the 	Objectivist
movement", 	wh ich 	lives on 	long after her
death .

People 	\Vith 	the 	most 	serious 	of fascination 	and
intrigue, 	even 	obsession 	with 	communication, 	persuasion,
influence; 	with 	psychology ....if that's you, 	then 	you 	will
\Velcome 	its 	challenging 	nature, 	appreciate 	being 	provoked,
and 	\till- 	I'm 	confident- 	agree 	with 	the 	Harrisons'
assessment; 	will 	rank 	it 	as one 	of 	the 	all-time 	bes.t 	investments
you've 	ever 	made 	\vith 	me.
•
If 	your 	interests are 	of 	a more 	superficial 	nature, and 	your
desire 	limited to simple 	shortcuts 	to 	sales, 	I'm 	afraid 	you '11 	be
disappointed.
Now, here are some 	of the 	specific 	''gems" 	you'll 	find 	in
this 	unique program. 	Please take a 	momen~ 	read 	them 	all- 	don't
skip 	over 	this 	or 	skim. Contemplate what incorporating this
know-how 	into 	ALL 	your writing might mean, 	in 	heightened
interest 	and 	impact, 	in 	response and results, 	in 	retention ....

- 	you 	- 	as 	Influencer
.
•Nineteen 	Principles 	f o r 	wr i 	ting
strategically- 	for 	every 	paragraph, 	a
pu~pose 	·
•FOK~LAS 	for 	INFLUENTIAL 	writing
•The 	power 	of 	FABLE 	and 	how 	t o 	use 	it
•Borrowed 	from 	Hollywood: 	HIGH 	CONCEPT
(every 	GREAT 	business 	has 	one)
•How 	your 	writing 	can 	elevate 	you 	to 	WELCOME
PROPHET 	STATUS
•Five 	TESTS 	to 	know 	if 	you 	are 	succeeding 	at
INFLUENTIAL 	writing
•How 	to 	move 	your 	readers/customers 	from
ordinary 	Interest 	to 	FASCINATION
•THE 	writing 	DEVICE 	that 	gets 	you 	past 	"what
gives 	him 	the 	right 	to 	tell 	me 	how 	to .... 	"
