•Why 	'story' 	trumps 	'facts', 	and 	how 	to 	use
'MENTAL 	IMPRINTING' 	to 	advantage
•Four 	key 	categories 	of 	Story . - 	building
your 	inventory-of-reliables 	in 	each 	category
•How 	to 	make 	people 	desire 	connection 	with
you 	(lessons 	from 	Martha 	Stewart, 	Howard
Stern, 	Rush 	Limbaugh 	and 	me ....and 	a 	TEMPLATE
Who 	has 	used
INFLUENTIAL 	Writing 	to
Great 	Purpose 	and 	Benefit?
At a time 	he 	was 	under attack 	as 	simple
pornographer, 	Hugh 	Hefner wrote a series
of articles 	defining 	and 	describing 	'The
Playboy 	Philosophy', 	which 	incorporated
libertarian and 	constitutionalist 	politics,
gender equality 	and 	feminism, 	aspirational
lifestyle, and 	other matters quite
disconnected 	from 	·pictures of naked
women"- and he succeeded 	at making
Playboy about something 	more important
than those 	pictures 	or a hedonist's magazine , 	.
and, 	to 	considerable 	extent, silencing 	cribcs.
Actress 	Suzanne Somers 	has 	used articles
and books 	on 	her controversial 	positions 	on
cancer 	prevention 	and 	treatment, 	causes 	of
disease, 	womens' 	health 	issues notably
including hormone therapy , etc. to 	estab lish
herself as a celebrated 	authority 	on
alternative 	health, 	as well 	as 	supporting 	a
multi-faceted 	business enterprise 	in 	that field
- 	despite having no 	formal 	tra ining 	or
academic credent ials in 	the field 	.
David 	Ogilvy was 	the 	first to 	set himself
apart from, 	and 	place himself on 	a higher
plane than 	his competitors 	in the 	advertising
fie ld, 	by writing 	a book 	about his philosophy
of advertising . He 	bui lt what was 	in its time
the largest and 	most prominent 	ad 	agency 1n
the world.
Martha Stewart has 	used 	her own
magazine, 	books, 	blog 	and 	other media 	to
champion 	an 	approach 	to 	living, 	lifestyle, 	and
home, 	it the 	basis for a giant commercial
enterprise incorporating 	licensing of her
brand 	to 	countless 	products 	and joint-
ventures 	with 	Macy's, 	Home 	Depot, 	and
others.
And, just in 	case 	you 	think 	this 	doesn't apply
to you 	and 	your 	'ordinary' 	business, 	consider
that 	much of her business has 	to 	do 	with
selling towels 	and 	toilet seat covers, 	dishes
and 	kitdlenware, 	and 	home furnishings.
These people 	used/use 	INFLUENTIAL
Writing to 	make themselves 	more important
and 	significant to 	people than 	'just another
merchant'; to 	connect with 	people 	as
advocates 	of philosophies; 	to 	influence
thinking, 	not just make sales. ,

Writing 	to 	Influence 	Thinking,
for 	Strategic 	Purpose
The week I was completing 	this l ette r, my friend
and speaking peer Jim Rohn passed away. 	Tens
of thousands of people - 	me included - 	have
been beneficially influenced by Ji m's wri tten
works and reco rdings.
His plain-spoken wisdom 	about personal
development: initiative, discipline, persistence
and work are universally meaningful, and 	can 	be
read, 	listened to, and 	enjoyed at face 	value.
However, 	if you 	understand 	their original
strategic purposes, you can see them 	in an
entirely different light, and 	appreciate their
construction in a different way .
Jim spent years almost entirely working within
the multi-level marketing/network marketing, as a
speaker, 	trainer and 	author, 	so his parables,
stories, lessons and material was 	all designed to
influence the thinking, attitudes and 	behavior of
the distributors and sales 	agents in ways the
parent companies needed them Influenced,
notably to 	discourage easy quitting, encourage
patience and persistence, 	immunize against
others' nega~ve criticism.
So , for example, 	Jim's messages about "planting
and -later- harvesting• have double meaning;
the 	apparen~ on-surface, 	universal meanings,
but also the underneath, specifically directed
message to MLM distributors about recruiting. Of
course, 	you have to 	have experiential
understanding of the 	MLM business to even
recognize the 	coded, 	purposeful messages
'hidden' in the bigger, broader message.
This is an important aspect of INFLU ENTIAL
Writi ng that I expl ore in my Program: the
construction and 	use of coded 	messages.
You're familiar with how dog wh isHes work: 	only
dogs hear them, 	people don't. Being
sophisticated enough 	to imbed 	·dog whistles·
into your writi ng , tha t on ly those 	you 	speci fi cal ly
seek con nection an d infl uence wit h, is an
advanced, high-level strategy an d skill you 	can
gain 	enormous benefit from having as your own .so ... f 	ro m 	one 	sentence 	you 	must 	listen 	for 	,
abou t 	what 	people 	think 	cult 	leaders 	do ,
bu t 	t h at 	is 	a 	mistake 	they 	actually 	do 	NOT 	make 	.
•BONUS: 	An 	in-depth 	description 	of 	the 	individua l
i !'lgr edi en t s 	I 	st r 	ive 	to 	include 	in 	every 	issue 	of 	The 	No
B .S. 	M	ark eting 	Letter 	: 	the 	J' checklist '' 	that 	governs 	i t s
co nte n t; 	t h e 	ten 	bases 	to 	touch 	every 	time 	.
-

So, 	should 	YOU 	invest 	in 	this 	Program?
If you 	have 	wondered 	what 	my 	objectives are, 	how 	my 	thoughts are organized,
how 	I 	prepare 	my 	newsletters, 	how ·I 	decide 	what must 	be 	included 	in 	every issue, 	why 	I
choose 	a topic .. .in 	short, 	"what 	I'm 	up 	to" 	underneath the words you 	read-: 	you'll 	find
that 	curtain 	pulled 	back 	and 	all revealed 	in 	this 	Program!
If you 	have ever envied 	th~ 	superior response and results that 	I've 	gotten 	for 	over
30 	years 	with 	my 	customers and that 	we 	now 	get at Glazer-Kennedy ..... and 	our 	results do
out-perforrn 	all 	others 	in 	any 	sixnilar 	businesses - such as high attendance 	at 	events 	when
others' 	are shrinking 	or 	canceling theirs; 	superior 	retention; ascension, etc., you 	will 	see
the 	'secrets' 	not 	only revealed, 	but 	listed 	in 	organized manner, 	and 	analyzed 	in 	this
Prograrn.
If you're cognizant 	of a 	'secret 	sauce' 	in 	my 	direct-response copywriting,
something deeper 	and·more 	powerful 	than 	the sales techniques 	that 	can 	be 	identified 	in
the 	work, 	and 	have 	productive curiosity 	about it, 	this Program makes 	it 	suddenly visible.
(There's 	a terrific scene in 	the 	mediocre movie 	made 	from the fabulous Walter Gibson novels 	of 	'The
Shadow', 	in 	which an entire, towering hotel that has existed 	but 	been invisible 	to 	everyone 	in 	the city with
'clouded 	minds' 	is suddenly visible 	only 	to 	The 	Shadow. This is the new, clearer understanding 	of why 	my
copy 	works 	so 	well- 	and 	why 	I'm paid 	such kingly sums to 	write 	it- 	this Program presents to 	you.)
If 	you 	recognize 	that 	maintaining a career, business, loyal, fascinated 	and
responsive audience 	for 	35 	years is a rare accomplishment, and 	you'd 	like to understand
- 	not just 	appreciate, 	but 	understand 	- 	how 	I've 	done 	it, 	I explain 	in this 	Program. (The
elite 	group 	at 	the live training 	all 	commented 	that I 	had 	revealed 	more 	"inside 	stuff' 	there
than 	they'd 	ever seen 	me 	do 	before - 	and that 	some 	thought imprudent. 	Even 	if 	you 	have
been 	with 	.me 	for 	years 	and 	have 	begun 	thinking 	you've. 	gpt _all 	I've 	got, 	you '11 	find 	pletJJY
fbat's 	"new'' 	in 	this 	Program~ 	If 	you 	are a die-hard Kennedy follower, you 	wouldn't 	want
to 	miss 	this 	for 	the 	world. 	If you 	happen 	to 	be 	relatively new, this can 	be 	a great
accelerator 	of 	your understanding.)
If you 	would 	like 	to 	dra•natically 	"elevate 	your 	garne" 	and 	create 	much
greater 	status 	for 	yourself 	in 	the 	•ninds 	of 	your 	clientele, 	then 	you 	must 	own 	and
inJanerse 	yourself 	in 	this. •
You 	will 	be 	taken deep inside a 	most 	thoughtful, deliberate, purposed 	and
psychological process 	of 	using the 	wri~n 	word. You will 	be 	fascinated. 	And
en1powered. •

LOOK 	OVER 	DAN'S 	SHOULDER
COPYWRITING 	/ 	MARKETING 	ExAMPLES
March 	2010
~ 2010//Dan 	S. Kennedy . Rights 	Reserved .
Published 	by 	Kennedy/LiDo 	Associates
FAX 330-922 ·9833 ••
In 	November 	of 	last 	year, 	I 	showed 	you 	pieces 	of 	copy 	from 	an 	ad 	for 	a 	client 	advertising 	a
pra-ctice-building 	system 	for 	chiropra.ctorst 	and 	in 	some 	month(s) 	before 	that, 	I shared 	pieces
of 	an 	ad 	for 	a 	different 	client 	selling 	a different 	practice-building 	system 	to 	that 	same
profession. 	In 	this 	issue, 	you 'll 	see 	both 	ads, 	complete, 	as 	they 	are 	now 	running 	in 	the 	trade
journal, 	DYNAMIC 	CHffi.OPRACTIC. 	So, 	you've 	seen 	some 	of 	the 	raw, 	rough 	copy leading 	up 	to
these 	befol!e. 	Here, 	now, 	the ftnjshed 	ads.
As 	a 	freelance 	copywriter, 	I 	often 	find 	myself 	writing 	copy 	for 	competimrs 	in 	the same
niche 	or 	product 	category. 	I never 	hide 	this 	reality. 	There 	have 	vecy, 	very 	rarely 	been 	any
clients prepared 	to 	buy 	non-compete 	exclusivity, 	which 	requires a 	minimum 	commitment 	of
$1-rnnlion 	per 	year. 	(Yes, 	we 	all 	know 	what I 	am, 	and 	that we're 	only 	h 	· 	over 	price.)
While 	knowing 	I'm 	working 	for 	competitors 	may 	be 	an 	emotional 	problem 	for 	a 	client 	or
potential 	client, 	it's rarely 	an 	actual, 	practical problem- 	in 	the rare 	instances 	when 	it 	is, 	I
voluntarily 	turn 	away 	a 	potential 	client. 	The 	answer 	to 	"why 	isn't 	it 	a 	problem? ... how 	can 	it
not 	be 	a 	problem?» 	is 	illustrated 	by 	these 	two 	ads, 	ma.king 	them 	worthy 	of 	thorough 	scrutiny;
tb.e 	answer 	holds_ 	a 	"golden 	keJ" 	to_you 	getting 	b~tter 	results 	from 	your 	own 	~vertising.
Would 	you 	like 	me 	to 	save 	you 	the 	brain-strain 	of 	figuring 	that answer 	out 	for 	yourself? 	Make
your study 	of 	these 	ads 	a 	verification 	and demonstration 	exercise rather 	than a 	difficult
investigation? 	I'll 	just 	bet 	you 	would! 	And, 	since 	I'm 	in 	an 	unreasonably, 	unseasonably 	good
mood 	this 	day, 	I 	will 	go 	ahead 	and 	spoil 	you 	with 	a 	reveal. 	Ready?
The 	answer 	is 	that 	no 	two 	competitors 	should 	be 	in~erchapgea.ble 	- 	but, jnstead, 	eacl} 	sho~d• 	• 	=
owp. 	and 	present 	their: 	own 	brHliantly 	sharpened, 	s~ecific, 	focused 	and 	(to 	greatest 	extent
humanly/creatively 	possible) 	unique 	POSITIONING.:....th~t 	will 	resonate 	s~ong!:y_Jiith 	some~
repel 	others 	-.as 	should 	your 	adv~~tising 	whether 	thez:~ 	w.:e_ concerns 	about 	(!Ompetitors 	or
not. 	The 	impact 	of 	competition 	is 	directly 	proportionate 	to the 	weakness, 	vagueness,
sameness 	and 	ordinariness 	of your 	positionjng vs. 	the 	strength, 	specificity, clarity 	of
differentiation 	of 	your 	positioning. 	My 	favorite 	Jack 	Trout quote 	is 	his 	book 	title: differentiate
- or 	die. 	If your 	positioning 	is 	weak, 	you 	must 	fear 	everyone. 	If your 	positioning 	is 	strong
enough, 	you 	need 	fear 	no 	one. 	Yea, 	you 	may 	walk 	confidently 	through 	the 	Valley 	of 	Death.....
We'll 	return 	to 	this, 	with 	Examples 	#1 	and 	#2, 	and 	an 	extra 	Example 	#3 	momentarily.
With 	Example 	#4, 	we 	look 	at 	something 	entirely different - a staple 	of direct-response 	oopy:
bullet 	points. 	If you 	can't 	write 	great 	bullets, 	you're 	in 	big 	trouble. 	So, 	some 	time 	spent 	on
this 	is 	always 	time 	well-spent.
Before 	we 	dig 	in, 	I want 	to 	remind 	you 	that 	you 	a.re 	viewi.ng 	work 	here 	that 	consumed 	weeks 	-
and 	a 	sizable 	portion 	of 	my 	wits 	(not 	replenishing 	that 	well 	at 	my 	age), 	and 	required
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 209 of 253 --

substantial 	investment 	by 	these 	clients. 	Please 	don't 	treat 	any 	of 	it 	casuaJJy. 	These 	days, 	we
all 	fJght 	~or 	every 	response 	we 	get, 	so 	even the littlest 	improvement, 	the 	tiniest 	edge 	matters.
These 	Examples 	offer 	you 	opportunity 	to 	find, 	extract 	and 	use 	something, 	to 	provide 	a
"bump" 	in 	your 	results.
EXAMPLE 	#1:
HV 	goss 	IS 	LOOI(tNa 	FOR •
•
The 	opening 	gambit 	here, 	borrowed 	from 	a number 	of 	previous 	uses, 	by 	me 	and 	by 	clients
(including 	Dennis 	Tubbergen 	with 	financial 	advisors; 	Jay 	Geier 	with 	doctors), 	dating 	way, 	way 	back
to 	the 	1980,s; 	it 	has 	a "feel" 	of 	a help 	wanted 	ad 	rather 	than 	a sales 	advertisement; 	the 	"tease" 	that
there 	may 	be 	patients 	waiting 	for 	assignment 	to 	one 	doc 	in 	an 	area. 	But 	as 	you 	read 	further, 	the
"thingn 	revealed 	is 	a conversion 	to 	a total 	pain 	management 	clinic, 	which 	means 	working 	with 	MD's
(considered 	'the 	enemy' 	by 	many 	DC's), 	requiring 	a 	very 	progressive, 	business-oriented 	DC. 	The
ad 	will 	repel 	most- 	note 	the 	"NOT 	a religious 	purisf 	line.
EXAMPLE#2:
OBAMA-CAR~
This 	ad 	is 	much 	more 	in-the-face 	about doom 	'n 	gloom 	and 	impending 	disaster. 	There 	is 	no
attempt 	to 	disguise 	it 	at 	b~ginning 	as 	anything 	but 	a sales 	pitch. 	Into 	it, 	you 	will 	see 	it 	is 	actually
about 	something 	quite 	different than 	the 	ad 	in 	Example 	#1; 	instead 	of 	a medical 	friendly, 	MD 	co-
operation 	approach, 	it 	is 	promoting 	a very 	non-medical, 	alternative 	health 	approach 	to 	practice
(·metabolic 	based" 	is 	code 	meaningful 	to 	these 	readers; 	"oxygen 	therapies"- 	kind 	of 	"out there",
fringe 	if 	not 	radical, 	and 	not 	at 	all 	favored 	by 	the 	medical 	establishment.)•
It 	is 	extremely 	unlikely 	that 	the 	same 	doctor 	could 	be 	intrigued 	by 	both 	these 	ads. 	Instead,
the 	doctor 	attracted 	to 	one 	is 	very 	likely 	to 	be 	repelled 	by 	the 	other. 	To 	whatever 	degree 	a doctor
might find 	one 	or 	the 	other 	of 	these 	appealing, 	he 	will 	likely
find 	the 	other just 	as 	uninteresting. 	lf 	he 	has 	.
strong 	positive 	reaction 	to 	one, 	he 	should 	have
strong 	negative 	reaction 	to 	the 	other. 	Since 	I wrote 	both,
that 	is 	intentional. 	It 	is 	my 	belief that 	each 	of 	these 	clients
needs 	to 	attract 	a doctor with 	a very 	particular 	mind-set
(each, 	different 	by 	extremes 	from 	the 	other) 	in 	order 	to •
DUierent
pomioni~Ut
anrads
dillermt
prospects.
succeed, 	so 	I 	have 	crafted 	each 	of 	these 	ads 	to 	attempt 	causing 	just 	the 	person 	with 	that 	mind ..set
to 	sit 	up 	and 	take 	notice .... while 	all 	others 	either 	ignore 	tt, 	easily 	shrug 	it 	off, 	or 	are 	offended 	and•
stay 	away. 	(To 	do 	this, 	my 	clients 	must 	be 	fairly 	sophisticated 	businesspeople, 	willing 	to 	eschew
quantity 	of 	response 	in 	favor 	of quality; 	to 	incur 	higher 	lead 	costs 	than 	might 	be 	achieved 	with 	a
'bigger tent• 	approach 	in 	exchange 	for 	avoiding 	waste 	of 	time 	and 	resources 	on 	poorly 	qualified
prospects, 	or 	prospects 	who 	are 	difficult 	to 	self 	or/and 	difficult 	to 	work 	with 	post-sale.)
Of course, 	you 	will 	find 	some 	common 	etements 	or 	similarities 	in 	both 	ads. 	There 	are 	enemies-
because 	selling 	against 	someone 	or 	something 	that 	threatens 	and/or 	angers 	the 	prospect, 	thus
aligning 	self with 	prospect, 	is 	a reliable 	and 	important tactic. 	There 	is 	promise 	of 	safety-security 	in
both, 	because 	everyone 	is 	anxious 	and 	nervous 	and 	unsettled 	in 	this 	economy, 	and 	in 	this
•
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 210 of 253 --

profession 	targeted 	with 	these 	ads, 	and 	feeling 	{more) 	secure 	is 	a universal 	desire. 	Also, 	in 	both
cases, 	at 	essence, 	a parental 	relationship 	is 	being 	sold, 	so 	prospects 	at 	least 	subconsciously
desirous 	of 	a parent 	and 	guardian 	are 	needed. 	The 	offers 	are 	basically 	the 	same: 	free 	info 	without
obligation 	(although 	you·n 	see 	one 	marketer 	offers 	different 	response 	options 	than 	the 	other.) 	But
the 	similarities 	and 	common 	elements 	are 	only 	instructional 	to 	the 	point 	that 	certain 	things 	are
universal 	essentials- 	in 	the 	same 	way 	a phone 	number is 	an 	essential 	element 	in 	a Yellow 	Pages
ad, 	particularly 	for 	plumbers 	and 	bail 	bondsmen. 	What 	is 	more 	instructive 	are 	the 	differences,
centered 	around 	each 	ad's 	unique 	positioning.
EXAMPLE 	#3
ettt 	Postpot1ed 	- 	Forever?
This 	ad 	also 	runs 	in 	the 	same 	trade 	pub 	(!) 	as 	Examples 	#1 	and 	#2, 	and 	is 	run 	by 	another 	client 	of
mine 	in 	this 	same 	field- 	one 	I've 	had 	enormous 	success 	with; 	he 	has 	gone 	from 	zero 	to 	over 	250
franchised 	clinics 	in 	just 	3 years, 	and 	made 	millions 	of dollars 	en-route, 	built 	and 	is 	building 	a very
valuable 	company, 	and 	delivering 	extraordinary 	value 	to 	his 	franchisees . 	I did 	not 	write 	this 	ad; 	he
did, 	although 	it 	borrows 	from 	copy 	I have 	written 	for 	him 	in 	the 	past. 	This 	ad 	is 	built 	on 	different
positioning 	than 	either 	of the 	other 	two. 	and, 	for 	the 	most 	part) will 	attract 	a different 	doctor 	than
either 	of 	the 	other 	two 	ads. 	(Thus, 	I don't 	think 	of them 	as 	''competing,. 	so 	much 	as 	"co-existing").
There 	are 	three 	things 	to 	nptice: 	first, 	this 	ad 	is 	aimed 	at 	a specific 	age 	demographic 	- 	the 	doctor
with 	some 	financial 	wherewithal 	and 	discipline 	(he 	has 	a 401 K- 	probably 	only 	20°/o 	do}, 	and 	he
has 	retirement 	in 	his 	thoughts. 	(Neither 	of 	the 	other 	two 	ads 	reference 	age 	or 	retirement.) 	Second,
this 	ad 	is 	for 	a franchise 	company, 	thus 	selling- 	and 	requiring- 	and 	much 	more 	bJatanHy 	offering
-a 	parent. 	Third, 	this 	ad 	is 	not 	openly 	about 	changing 	the 	kind 	of chiropractic 	care 	or 	health 	care
services 	offered 	(as 	are 	the 	other 	two 	ads, 	in 	different 	ways). 	Instead, 	this 	is 	about 	better 	business
management as 	much 	as 	anything. 	The 	testimonials 	are 	an 	about 	th~s. 	So 	this 	calls 	out 	to 	a doctor
with 	a certain 	mind-set 	and 	certain 	perceived 	needs.
KEY 	IDEAS:
r 	be 	from 	all 	· 	1: 	I 	think 	you 	need 	to 	liberate 	yourseH 	(or the
client 	you're writing 	copy 	for) 	from 	fear 	and 	obsession 	and 	worry 	about 	competition, 	as 	well
as 	protect 	yourself 	from 	actual 	impact of 	direct 	competition, 	in 	the 	only 	way 	that 	really 	works:
carving 	out some 	•space., 	in 	a marketplace 	or prospect 	group's
mind-space 	that 	you 	can 	own, 	thus 	positioning 	yourself
distinctively 	different 	from 	any 	and 	all. 	This 	is 	no 	time 	to 	be
out there 	as 	JUST 	a better option. 	I 	am 	making 	this 	point 	relentlessly,
everywhere 	I can. 	2: 	The 	best 	positioning 	is 	driven 	by 	the 	specifac;
VERY 	specific 	prospect 	or 	customer 	you 	wish 	to 	attract 	and 	are,
in 	fact, 	best suited 	to 	do 	well 	with ....therefore 	NOT 	driven 	by
whatever 	goods 	you've 	got 	in 	the 	back 	room 	you 	wa1t 	to 	sell.
3: 	You 	need 	to 	critically 	question 	your 	positioning,
llliSIS
l8be
illI
a leiter
particularly 	with 	regard 	to 	clear, 	forceful 	differentiation, 	and 	specifiC 	relevance 	and 	attractiveness 	to
. (If you 	are 	writing 	copy 	for 	clients, 	you 	can11et 	your 	starting 	point 	be 	their
present positioning, 	without this 	critical 	analysis.)

www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 211 of 253 --

EXAMPLE 	#4:
Bullets
This 	is 	draft 	copy 	for 	the 	back 	cover 	of 	a forthcoming 	book 	I've 	co-authored: 	MAKING 	THEM
BELIEVE- 	The 	21 	Principles 	& Lost 	Secrets 	of J.R. 	Brinkley-Style 	Marketing. 	The 	book 	features
the 	advertising 	and 	marketing 	of 	the 	doc 	selling 	goat testicle 	transplants, 	profiled 	in 	the 	N.Y. 	Times
bestselling 	novel, 	Charlatan. 	·
There 	are 	just eight 	bullets 	here. 	Often 	in 	ads, 	and 	even 	more 	so 	in 	sales 	fetters, 	you'll 	find/use
long, 	long 	lists 	of 	bullet 	points- sometimes 	pages 	of them. 	With 	something 	like 	a 	book 	cover,
space 	is 	at 	premium, 	so 	choosing 	wisely 	becomes 	more 	criticaL 	When 	space 	is 	not 	so 	restricteQ, 	I
p~en 	see 	"lazy 	.copyw:ri~ngn; 	out of 	100 	bullets, 	20 	are 	strong , unique , 	benefit 	rich; 	80 	are 	mundane
and 	generic 	and 	need 	cut 	or 	re-written. 	Like 	every 	other 	copywriter, 	I'd 	rather 	have 	room 	to 	roam
and 	even 	the 	luxury 	of slop, 	but 	I very 	often 	work 	in 	environments 	where 	there 	are 	strict 	and
inflexible 	space 	limitations: 	book 	cover, 	TV 	infomercial, 	postcard , full 	page 	magazine 	ad. 	tn 	these
environments, 	you 	haggle 	with 	self 	or 	others 	over 	even 	a single 	word. 	Each 	of 	these 	eight, 	then ,
tries 	getting 	a lot 	done 	with 	a little.
OVerall, 	the 	object is to convey there 	is 	real 	"meat" 	in 	this 	book. 	Things 	to 	use. 	Thus, 	"tvvo 	kinds 	or,
"THE 	question", 	"3-Step•, 	"the 	best answer". 	That there 	is 	one 	or 	Oust) 	two 	or 	three 	items 	suggests 	both
specificity 	and 	ease 	of Jeaming 	and 	use. 	NOTE : you 	should 	have 	a clear, 	over-arching 	objective 	for your 	list
of buUets .... what 	single, 	overall 	idea 	or 	impression 	do 	you 	want 	people 	to 	come 	away 	with 	as 	result 	of
reading 	them 	all?)
Four of the eight include specific promises of outcome 	benefits. 	(# •s 	1, 3, 	6, 	8). 	The 	others 	aUude 	to
secrets 	you'll 	discover, two 	of 	which 	are 	'negatives' 	to 	avoid. 	One 	deals 	with 	the 	plague 	that 	strikes 	terror
into 	more 	sellers' 	hearts 	than 	anything 	else: 	price 	objections.
Seven 	of 	the 	eight 	are 	two part 	bullets; 	basically, a feature, 	then 	a benefit. 	This 	represents 	care 	taken
in 	structure. 	There •s 	also 	care 	in 	word 	choice. 	Words 	like 	dynamic; 	dramatically; 	elevates; 	embrace; 	virtually
unlimited; 	espec;a/ly eager are 	all 	result of weighing 	several 	possible 	options.
With 	something 	like 	a book, 	the 	source 	material 	for 	the 	buUets 	is 	right 	at 	your 	fingertips- 	in 	the 	table 	of
contents, 	chapter titJes , and 	content 	itself. 	With 	other 	ltinds of products, 	say a 	bottle 	of glop, 	it's 	not so
handy; 	you 	have 	to 	go 	through 	all 	the 	prior 	advertising, 	competitors' 	advertising, 	oJd 	ads 	in 	swipe 	files,
technical 	product 	infonnation, 	testimonials, 	maybe 	talk 	to 	customers, 	salespeople. 	Pulling 	buDets 	from 	info-
products 	can 	be 	a breeze 	compared 	to 	writing 	them 	for a lawn 	mower 	or 	a nutritional 	supplement. 	To 	do
these, 	I wrote 	about 	twenty 	to 	produce 	these 	eight, 	and 	I re-wrote 	and 	tightened 	all 	eight from 	the 	first
version. 	It took 	about an 	hour, 	start 	to 	finish.
You'll 	also 	see 	a tine 	highlighting 	special 	items 	included 	in 	the 	book, that 	add 	vaJue 	and 	wit I be 	of interest-
to 	such 	an 	extent that 	people 	who 	might 	never 	read 	the 	entire 	book 	would 	want 	to 	have 	those 	items. 	There 's
also 	something 	I use 	a lot with 	info-products: 	name-dropping. 	A list of 	people 	who 	appear 	in 	the 	book. 	who
may 	be 	familiar, 	and 	about whom 	people 	have 	known, 	existent 	curiosity and 	interest.
Tba.t's 	plenty 	for 	one 	month, 	don't 	ya 	thjnk? 	DSK -
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

A{JDI(),_VIDEO, 	ONLINE 	PRESENTA'I'ION 	SCRIPTS2& 	v 	a 	s 	a 	a 	s 	So 	a 	m 	u 	= 	r 	0
•Audio 	CD- 	to include 	\\rith 	print material
•Audio 	CD 	to 	send separately
• Audio 	CD 	as 	referral tool
•O.nline 	Audio Presentation 	I 	Tele-Seminar
• DVD 	- 	to 	include 	~rith 	print 	material
•DVD 	to send separately
•DVD 	as 	referral 	tool
•Online 	DVD 	Presentation 	I 	Webinar
•
Reasons:
•Different 	people 	accept ideas best via different 	media
•Multi-media 	reinforcement
•Potential for repetition
•Can 	LISTEN 	non-exclusively/conveniently
•Many 	more 	likely 	to WATCH 	TV 	than 	read
•Drama •
•Demonstration
•Differentiation
•Authority (via 	format, 	host, etc.)
•
Case 	for 	SCRIPTING:
•
Egs.: 	Now~ 	every restaurant 	owner 	slaps up photos 	or 	video 	of their restaurant 	on 	their 	web 	site.
But 	hardly 	anybody has a 	sales 	script written 	by 	a pro, then the video 	and 	images created 	to 	fulfill
the 	script, so that 	the 	viewer has a 	sense 	of tuning 	into a mini- TV 	report about 	the 	place, and has
excitement created 	by 	the 	words 	of the reporter, interviews with owner, chef, customers.
(Variation, courtesy Halbert: 	Imagine- 	and think 	how 	valuable 	it 	might 	be 	- 	if the 	locaJ 	nightly
news 	show 	sent a film 	crew 	and 	host 	out 	to 	produce a 	story 	about 	your 	business? 	They 	aren't, 	but
we 	can ... )
Facilitating Sale:
Ego/Competitiv~ness- 	competing peer, industry leader has and he 	doesn't
Plan for 	Use 	- 	many 	ways 	to 	use and profit
Multi-pw:pose 	the 	content, so 	fee 	amortizes better
Area _exclusivity
Can a 	sppnsor(s) 	be 	recruited 	to offset cost 	and/or assist 	with 	distribution/promotion
From 	Writer 	to 	Producer:
•1-Stop 	Shop- 	no 	confusion, 	difficulty for client
•Correct person in 	control
•More 	money- 	package price
WHO?
•Any 	business 	that 	generates leads, 	has follow-up 	steps 	but doesn't use tele-
seminar/webinar, online 	audio/video, CD/DVD, 	etc.
•Person relying on expert positioning

-- 223 of 253 --

Situations
1: 	You 	create sales tools, to be used in field, 	in 	store, 	in 	advance 	of or 	in 	support 	of the
sale. There will 	be 	staff/salesforce resistance to its use. You can provide an "approach"
to deter 	that resistance and 	encourage 	willing 	use 	of the new tools. ( egs. 	Survey/1-1
Interviews 'Before'; 	Installation 	Presentationi+Contest; 	Follow-up 	Training 	x# 	days
afterward.)
2: 	You 	create a "copy 	bank" 	that 	in-house writers are going to draw from and use in
pumping out daily e-mails, tweets, grunt-work promotions, etc. for a long time to come.
If the "bank" is just 	dumped on them, there will 	be 	resistance, sabotage, non-use. 	You
can provide an "approach" to deter than resistance.
3: 	You 	develop a group presentation script for a company's speaker. 	You 	can provide
role-play coaching, video critique and in-field implementation.
Other 	Speaking 	I 	Training 	Oppo1·tunities
•In--house 	copywriters: training, coaching, critiques
•Marketing 	teams: 	training, brainstorming sessions
•Creativity workshops
•Theory, 	Psychology 	& 	Principles 	of 	Direct-Response
•Other 	specific topics ....re. social media, newsletters, etc.
•For 	business owners: 	Uncovering 	Hidden Opportunities
IF 	SPEAKING 	FREE 	OR 	LOW 	FEE 	TO 	CLUBS, 	ASSOCIATIONS, 	BUSINESS
GROUPS:
Have a product to sell linked to your speech (and insist on selling 	it)*
Plant 	seeds re. the work you 	do- 	show 	'n 	tell, case histories
Tell what to do/what not to do, not how to
Drive home feelings 	of inadequacy
The 	''why 	aren,t 	we 	doing 	that?" 	reaction
*If you're bad, there's one 	in 	every crowd. 	If you get 	"okay", 	l/4th 	the room. 	If you get
goo~ 	up 	to~.
CONSIDER 	CREATING AND 	PROMOTING 	YOUR 	OWN 	SEMINAR/WORKSHOP
FOR 	INCO:ME 	& 	CLIENT 	ACQUISITION
What 	to 	sell via this means -
Product(s)
Term coaching/mastermind group
On-going mastermind 	group

PIGURE 	4-'1. 	While 	his "Essential 	Salon 	Owners 	Marketing 	Toolkitn includes
training on produdng newsletters 	for 	salons, many 	of Greg 	Milner's 	cus-
tomers aren't 	able to 	produce a 	newsletter 	for 	themselves 	every 	month..
I herefore, Greg 	allows 	his 	dients 	to 	enroll 	in 	an 	automatic 	service 	that 	pub-
lishes 	a 	newsletter 	automatically for 	his 	clients' customers. This 	program 	is 	a
large 	profit center for 	Greg, and 	it 	benefits 	his 	clients 	because 	their 	customers
retum more frequently and 	buy 	more products. To 	review 	complete 	samples.
of Greg's 	marketing 	tnaterials 	visit 	wwwJnfoMarketing 	Milner.

Smart 	Tip
People 	will 	buy 	the 	• •
choose to 	use, 	the 	steps to producing 	your
information 	product are 	the 	same, 	so 	once
you've 	made 	your 	first 	product, 	you'll 	be able 	to
make your next 	one 	even faster.
To 	help 	you 	visualize what 	an 	inforn1ation
product looks 	like, 	let me summarize 38 different
ways 	you 	can combine 	audio, video, 	or printed
infonnation 	into 	popular selling products .
same 	information in
multiple formats, 	so 	you 	can
offer the inform anon 	on
audio, 	in 	printed 	reports and
workbooks, and 	in 	a 	video.
•

3w 	Mlmualsfworkbooks. 	Typically 	50 	or 	more 	pages published 	in 	a three-ring

___me
ven 	though 	you 	have 	a fantastic 	website,
you~l/ 	still 	have 	a 	lot 	of visitors who don't 	buy.lt)s 	important

iarity 	An 	ezine 	is 	a 	great 	way 	to 	increase 	the value 	ofyour

-- 245 of 253 --

-
124
•
zines 	(e-mail 	newsletters) 	can 	prove 	to 	be 	a 	surprisingly valuable 	method 	for 	mar-
~keting your 	info-products 	online. 	If you 	already 	distribute 	to 	a 	mailing 	list 	or have
considered 	it 	as 	an 	option 	for 	your 	info-product 	business, 	you 	are 	already well 	on 	your
way 	to 	boosting 	your 	sales 	quickly 	and 	easily 	by 	using 	an 	ezine.
So, 	what 	is 	an 	ezine? 	Basically any 	e-mail 	publication 	that 	your 	readers 	subscribe
or 	"opt 	in" 	to 	because 	they're 	interested 	in 	the 	infor1nation 	you're 	sharing. 	Ideally,
you 	want your 	ezine 	to 	be 	related 	to 	the 	content 	in your 	info-products; 	that 	way 	your
subscribers 	are 	an 	ideal 	message-to-market 	match 	for 	your 	business.
And 	you 	want 	your 	ezine 	to 	be FREE. 	Why? 	Most 	people 	are 	online 	to 	search 	for
infortnation, 	not 	to 	buy 	something. 	They 	may 	end 	up 	buying 	something, 	but 	first
they'll 	always 	look 	for 	free 	information. 	So, 	your 	ezine 	is 	the 	bait. 	By 	offering 	FREE
information 	on 	the 	topic 	of your 	inforrnation 	product, 	they 	will 	get 	on 	your 	mailing
list, 	hear 	from 	you 	every 	week 	or 	so, 	and 	eventually 	want 	to 	buy.
Also, 	by 	building 	a 	large 	list 	of people 	who 	are 	interested 	in 	your 	specific 	topic 	of
choice, 	you 	g-ain 	years 	of future 	income 	oppor-
tunity. 	How? 	Because 	now 	you 	have 	built-in
buyers. 	And 	over 	time, 	you 	can 	introduce 	new
products 	and 	sernces 	you 	lmow 	they 	will 	be
interested 	in. 	You'll 	find 	your 	list 	~'ill 	be 	the
most 	important 	asset 	you'll 	actually 	build 	with
your 	infortnation 	marketing 	business. 	The 	who
is 	actually 	more 	important 	than 	the 	what.
I 	started 	my 	first 	ezine several years 	ago 	to
help 	promote 	my 	marketing 	and 	copywriring
services 	when 	I lived 	in 	New 	York City. 	(It 	was
called 	Straight 	Shooter Marketing, 	and I 	still
publish 	it 	today, 	with 	more 	than 	24,000 	sub-
Bri.ght 	Idea
List 	building, by 	far, 	is
one 	of the 	most 	effec-
tive 	means of marketing to a
selected 	niche 	or 	target 	audi-
ence. 	Because your 	subscribers
have expressed an 	interest 	in
your 	ezine, they 	will 	typically
also 	be 	interested 	in 	your
inforrnation 	products 	and
•
serVlces.
scribers.) 	When 	I 	grew 	tired 	of 	working 	hours 	for 	dollars 	and 	learned 	more 	about
info-marketing, 	I decided 	to 	launch 	my 	first 	info-product 	on 	a 	topic 	I 	had 	become•
knowledgeable 	about 	ezine 	publishlng! 	(Figure 	9.1 shows a 	recent 	issue 	of my 	ezine
today.)
My 	first 	ebook 	was called 	Boost 	Business 	With 	Your 	Own 	Ezine, 	and 	once 	I 	had 	it
ready, 	along 	with 	a 	simple 	web 	page 	where 	people 	could 	learn 	more 	and 	purchase, I
thought, 	"Gee, 	I 	wonder 	if 	my 	ezine 	readers 	would 	be 	interested 	in 	this?" 	So, 	I 	sent
them 	an 	e-mail 	about 	it. 	WlL-'\.M 	. . . 	that 	was 	the 	day my 	life 	changed 	forever. 	As 	I
watched 	the 	orders 	come 	into 	my 	e-mail 	in box, 	one 	after 	the 	other, I realized I 	wanted
to 	be 	a 	full-time 	information 	marketer.
Today, I 	make 	millions 	of 	dollars a 	year 	from 	infonnation 	marketing, 	and 	I 	think
it's 	the 	best 	business 	in 	the 	world! I 	work 	out 	of 	mv 	beachfront 	home 	in 	SouthernJ
•
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

Refer 	to 	book: 	THE 	OFFICIAL 	GET 	RICH 	GUlDE 	TO 	INFORMATION 	MARKE
California and travel 	wherever 	I like 	since all 	I 	need 	to 	work 	is 	my 	laptop. I set 	my
own 	schedule 	and take 	multiple 	vacations a 	year. 	I 	don't 	have 	any 	full-time 	employees
or 	an 	office. 	And 	not 	only 	is 	it 	a lucrative business, 	but 	I'm 	helping thousands 	of peo-
ple 	in 	the process by 	delivering helpful inforrnation 	to 	change 	their 	businesses 	and
lives. (And you 	will, 	too!)
So, 	I've 	obviously figured 	out 	a few things 	that 	I'm 	doing 	the 	right 	way, 	and 	I'll 	tell
you, my 	number-one tool in building 	this 	business 	has 	·been 	that 	very 	simple ezine.
You 	should start publishing 	one 	ASAP. 	This 	chapter will 	tell 	you 	how.
even 	a 	san 	Ezine 	i 	He 	You
a e 	More 	Mone	e 	More 	an •
1n•
Your 	In 	orrnation 	Mar 	etin 	Business
There 	are 	dozens 	of different 	ways 	an ezine 	will benefit 	your 	information product
business, 	but 	below 	are 	the most 	important 	seven. 	"Which 	of 	these 	business-gro,ving
benefits 	best 	appeals 	to 	your 	goals? 	.
1. 	Ezines 	Make 	It 	Possible 	for 	You 	to 	Grow 	a
Lengthy 	Mailing 	List 	That 	You 	Can
Market 	to 	Over 	and 	Over 	Again
Rather than 	marketing 	one 	to 	one, 	you can expand 	your 	marketing 	reach to hrm-
dreds, thousands, 	or even millions 	of 	potential 	customers 	all 	at one time. 	Once 	you
begin 	to 	build 	a 	mailing list that you can market through, 	you 	will find yourself with
a 	lot 	more time 	on 	your hands than before. 	By 	offering a 	&ee 	ezine, 	you 	make 	it 	pos-
~
•
... ~
.• 	••
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
••.
•
sible 	to 	collect large 	amounts 	of 	contact information from curre.	nt 	and prospective 	·
customers, allowing you 	to 	market 	to 	them more effectively than before. 	In 	every issue
of your 	ezine, 	you 	will be able 	to 	share 	infottnation 	about your 	inforrnation 	products
with 	your 	target audience over and over 	again.
2. 	Ezine~ 	Provide 	an 	Extremely 	Effective 	Means 	for
Promoting 	Your 	Info-Products
Rather 	than 	simply 	bragging 	about 	how 	great 	your 	productS 	are 	(a..c; 	traditional
advertising tends to do), marketing via an e7ine allows 	y~u 	to demonstrate how great
